imaging.name: Imaging
imaging.definition: A structured, sensory-rich mental visualization process in which the actor creates vivid internal experiences — seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and feeling — with the purpose of stimulating authentic emotional life, communicating with the unconscious, and fulfilling acting obligations.
imaging.details: Morris distinguishes imaging from casual daydreaming or 'head-only' visualization. Imaging must involve all five senses simultaneously, not just the visual. The image should leave the 'inner screen' and surround the actor as a living reality. Morris positions imaging as Choice Approach #26 in his system, and calls it the 5th MEGAPPROACH (and possibly a SUPERMEGAPPROACH). Imaging can be used instrumentally (to free the actor's instrument) and as craft (to fulfill scene obligations). When practiced repeatedly, images sink into the unconscious and influence behavior, dreams, and character. External research: Olympic ski-team study showed imagined practice outperformed physical-only practice; medical studies showed imaging increased white blood cell counts; sports psychologists achieved 15% improvement in free-throw accuracy via imaging.

sources.summary: Introduction: article on medical imaging sparked Morris's 10-year exploration of imaging as acting tool.
Relaxation is the universal starting point; Morris disputes the 'eyes closed only' convention.
Imaging is Choice Approach #26; also useful instrumentally and for communicating with/programming the unconscious.
Imaging is natural since infancy; it includes all five senses, not just visual.
All imaging practitioners agree on starting with relaxation; Morris advocates combining imaging with Sense Memory.
Imaging is the 5th MEGAPPROACH, potentially a SUPERMEGAPPROACH — most powerful tool in the system.

the_instrument.name: The Instrument
the_instrument.definition: The actor's entire being — body, emotions, senses, impulses, psyche — which must be freed of obstacles, inhibitions, and tensions before authentic acting is possible.
the_instrument.details: Morris's system begins with instrumental work because a blocked actor cannot create truth. Obstacles include fear, tension, self-consciousness, low self-esteem, social conditioning. Over 1,000 instrumental exercises developed in Morris's 37+ years of teaching. Basic preparation: achieve relaxation → elevate sensory accessibility → contact moment-to-moment feelings → freely express impulses. Instrumental work uses the Relaxation Cluster: relaxation technique + Sensitizing + Personal Inventory.

sources.summary: The system has two parts: the instrument and the craft.
Instrumental work eliminates fears one by one via direct confrontation; domino effect — addressing one fear causes others to dissipate.
Relaxation Cluster (relaxation + Sensitizing + Personal Inventory) is the first preparatory step.

the_craft.name: The Craft
the_craft.definition: The technical process by which an actor addresses dramatic material and fulfills it — comprising obligations, choices, and choice approaches — used to stimulate the character's emotional life authentically.
the_craft.details: Craft breaks down into: (1) Obligation — what the actor must experience; (2) Choice — the object/person/place/thing selected to stimulate that experience; (3) Choice Approach — how the actor creates the choice as a reality. There are 7 major obligations: Time and Place, Relationship, Emotional, Character, Historic, Thematic, Subtextual. There are 27 choice approaches (Imaging is #26). The craft is worked while saying lines; the actor creates the choice simultaneously with the text.

sources.summary: Craft is the technique for stimulating emotional life; divided into obligations, choices, and choice approaches.
Summary: obligation = what to experience; choice = what will stimulate it; choice approach = how to create the choice as reality.

obligations.name: Obligations
obligations.definition: The seven major responsibilities an actor must address when approaching dramatic material — the specific experiences the character must have as defined by the script.
obligations.details:
1. Time and Place
2. Relationship
3. Emotional
4. Character
5. Historic
6. Thematic
7. Subtextual
Each obligation requires the actor to select a choice from his own life experience that parallels the demand of the material.

sources.summary: Seven major obligations listed; each requires a parallel choice from the actor's life.

choices.name: Choices
choices.definition: An object, person, place, or thing (including odors, weather conditions, sounds) selected from the actor's own life to address a specific obligation of the material and stimulate the desired emotional response.
choices.details: Choices are not decisions to do something — they are always objects in the broad sense. Selected with knowledge of what they mean emotionally to the actor. If a choice fails to stimulate the desired response it is discarded for another. Examples: photographs, music, a letter, someone you love, an odor, an environment, a memory.

sources.summary: A choice is an object, not an action decision; selected from the actor's life to stimulate the required emotional response.
Infinite possibilities for choices; used to address all seven obligations.

choice_approach.name: Choice Approach
choice_approach.definition: The method by which an actor creates a chosen object/experience as a tangible reality — making the choice real enough to genuinely affect him during a scene.
choice_approach.details: There are 27 choice approaches in Morris's system (Imaging = #26). Selected based on what will most efficiently create the specific choice. Examples: Sense Memory, Imaging, Evocative Words, Available Stimulus, inner monologue. Selecting the right choice approach is a result of experience and experimentation.

sources.summary: Choice approach is how the actor creates the choice — makes it real. There are 27, each suited to different circumstances.
The actor works the choice approach while saying lines and expressing all impulses.

involuntary_imaging.name: Involuntary Imaging
involuntary_imaging.definition: Mental images that arise automatically and without premeditation — spontaneous pictures, fragments, and sensations that occur during idle, bored, anxious, or dreaming states.
involuntary_imaging.details: Occurs when bored, idle, or anxious, or when something barely perceptible triggers image chains. These images come from the unconscious and carry meaning; they can illuminate what the actor feels about a character. In performance, involuntary images stimulated by choices during rehearsal can be re-created as a richer substitute for the original choice. Morris class experiment: actors re-creating involuntary images found them produced richer, more dimensional emotional life than original choices. Theory: the unconscious translates conscious choices into its own pictorial language, yielding images that address the obligation more deeply.

sources.summary: Involuntary Imaging occurs without premeditation; daydreaming, boredom, anxiety trigger it.
In acting, Involuntary Imaging helps discover new choices, explore characters, and access unconscious support.
Class experiment: re-creating involuntary images produced more dimensional emotional life than working directly for the original choice.

additions.note: Involuntary Imaging is specifically recommended as a technique for unconscious access because there is no direction or premeditation involved—the actor starts with an empty blackboard and allows whatever arises. Morris's example: starting with 'nothing,' images of roasting marshmallows, childhood in a vacant lot, Bobby Snell, war movies, and a bicycle ('Pacemaker') arose spontaneously, releasing a powerful well of unconscious memory. Distinct from other techniques: can be used to explore the unconscious OR to prime unconscious support for a specific acting obligation.

symbolic_imaging.name: Symbolic Imaging
symbolic_imaging.definition: Images that arise as symbols — often abstract or non-literal — typically from the unconscious during sleep-wake states, dreams, or even waking consciousness, carrying meaning beyond their surface appearance.
symbolic_imaging.details: Common in dreams; the unconscious uses its own symbol language (per Jung: 'Images are the language of the unconscious'). Symbolic images can attach to other image types. An actor can deliberately re-create a symbolic image to access the emotional state associated with it. Symbols can function as emotional 'triggers' — e.g., a color associated with a place can instantly restimulate the feelings of that place. Morris recommends actors keep a journal to track recurring symbolic images.

sources.summary: Symbolic Imaging occurs in sleep-wake states or dreams; symbols are often hard to interpret but carry unconscious meaning.
Symbols can function as triggers — re-creating a symbolic image instantly accesses its associated emotional state.
Keep journals to log, track, and interpret symbolic images for future use as choices.

additions.content: Six specific uses of Symbolic Imaging: (1) learning tool for self-understanding; (2) discovering new, impacting choices; (3) supporting the creative/imaginative process; (4) discovering new areas of affectability; (5) reaching emotional places inaccessible to more conscious approaches; (6) reaching and communicating with the unconscious. Morris recommends being especially attentive to symbolic images that arise while working on a specific role — sleep-based images during that period are particularly relevant.

additions.note: Symbolic Imaging directly solicits unconscious activity and is recommended in Chapter 6 as one of the primary techniques for establishing communication with the unconscious. Refer to Chapter 3 for the full technique description.

fragmented_imaging.name: Fragmented Imaging
fragmented_imaging.definition: A form of imaging in which the actor generates a rapid, non-sequential stream of disconnected images — with or without a theme — allowing impressions to tumble freely without needing to connect chronologically.
fragmented_imaging.details: Can be done with a theme (e.g., 'I am on the beach with someone') or completely impulsively. Images need not connect or make sense — impressionistic torrent. Useful for stimulating imagination, exploring character, and as a preparation.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging: rapid, non-sequential image stream with or without a theme; images need not connect logically.

additions.content: Fragmented Imaging is done completely by design — actor decides on a theme and encourages images. It can address all seven obligations. Compared to Sensory Suggestion vs. Sense Memory: Fragmented Imaging is to Imaging what Sensory Suggestion is to Sense Memory — faster, less elaborate, useful for speedy transitions. Key distinction from Free-Association Imaging: Fragmented Imaging involves creative direction and selective emphasis of images; Free-Association Imaging is completely hands-off, accepting whatever arrives without steering.

free_association_imaging.name: Free-Association Imaging
free_association_imaging.definition: A deliberate imaging practice in which the actor relaxes, blanks the mind, and allows whatever images arise to surface without control or direction — an intentional version of involuntary imaging used for exploration and preparation.
free_association_imaging.details: Used by psychotherapists to help clients understand their own obstacles and psychology. As acting tool: stimulates imagination, works as involvement exercise, primes the actor for more organized imaging. Differs from Involuntary Imaging in that it is initiated by conscious choice, then released.

sources.summary: Free-Association Imaging: done by design — relax, blank out, let images arise freely; used by therapists and actors for exploration.

additions.content: Six specific rewards of Free-Association Imaging: (1) becomes more facile with imaging generally; (2) very good involvement preparation — takes you away into selfless-involvement state; (3) elevates affectability and emotional energy; (4) tremendous insights into yourself; (5) wonderful way to discover new choices and unexplored areas of life; (6) connects to unconscious impulses and opens doors to unconscious dimension. Important during rehearsal: imaging free associatively while in rehearsal can liberate images from the unconscious that support the character journey. Morris's own taped example demonstrates how the exercise surfaced childhood memories, insights about fears, and love for his father — leaving him vulnerable, nostalgic, and ready to act.

story_imaging.name: Story Imaging
story_imaging.definition: The most conventional form of imaging — following a logical sequence of events or narrative through visualized experience, including daydreaming and fantasizing.
story_imaging.details: Most people do this naturally. Can be done consciously (with leadership) or less-than-consciously. Daydreams and fantasies are typically Story Imaging. Often used when Verbalized Imaging is applied — narrating a story through images.

sources.summary: Story Imaging follows a logical narrative sequence; daydreaming and fantasizing are forms of it.

verbalized_imaging.name: Verbalized Imaging
verbalized_imaging.definition: Imaging done out loud — making the image process audible, narrating the experience as it unfolds so the actor also hears the images, adding auditory reinforcement to the visual.
verbalized_imaging.details: Encourages use of the auditory sense alongside the visual. Acts as self-discipline — prevents mental wandering. Often used in Story Imaging context. Can be done solo or in pairs (Two-Person Verbalized Imaging).

sources.summary: Verbalized Imaging makes imaging audible — actor hears as well as sees the images; good discipline that prevents drifting.

additions.content: Seven uses of Verbalized Imaging: (1) as its own approach accompanying any form of imaging; (2) as a discipline to start the imaging process when stuck; (3) to stay on track when wandering; (4) to supplement images with sounds that dimensionalize sensory responses; (5) as a device to access parts of already-created events via NLP-like trigger words; (6) to stimulate emotional expression during the imaging; (7) to pique unconscious responses by verbalizing dream or involuntary images. Important note: when verbalizing images, do NOT respond in words — keep sensory responses non-verbal or the process short-circuits into mere description. Verbalized Imaging is particularly powerful as a shortcut — after creating a full imaging experience multiple times, just a few verbalized images (e.g. 'I smell her rotten breath') can restimulate the entire emotional state.

guided_imaging.name: Guided Imaging
guided_imaging.definition: An imaging process facilitated by a guide (self or partner) who suggests elements, asks questions, or leads the imager through an experience — also called Facilitated Imaging.
guided_imaging.details: Can be done solo (actor guides himself) or with a partner who facilitates. Facilitator suggests elements of an event or asks sensorial questions the imager responds to. Variants: Two-Person Guided Imaging, Simultaneous Guided Imaging, Shared Imaging. Used both as craft preparation and as a tool for exploring relationships.

sources.summary: Guided (Facilitated) Imaging: a guide leads the imager through an experience by suggesting elements or asking sensorial questions.

additions.content: Five uses of Guided Imaging: (1) involvement preparation; (2) relationship preparation — actors become deeply involved in each other's lives; (3) way of exploring a choice without anticipatory bias (facilitatee cannot predict facilitator's questions); (4) practice and mastery of imaging process; (5) choice approach to fulfill scene obligations. The facilitator must know all elements of the actor's experience in detail. Process resembles Affective Memory approach: establish surrounding realities first, then reach the impelling core. Three sub-variants: Guided Imaging (one leads other), Simultaneous Guided Imaging (both lead each other), Shared Imaging (one describes, other images). Shared Imaging builds shared history and can be done scene-by-scene through a play, also covers extra-textual life. If Shared Imaging explorations are done in a hypnagogic state, they may slip into dreams and create unconscious connections between the actors.

Detailed facilitation example (pp. 134-135): facilitator moves through establishing environment, objects, temperature, sounds, then introducing the other person — how they look, how they're dressed, what they're doing — then moving to contact, approach, physical proximity, emotional response, verbal exchange. The facilitatee responds to every question with full Imaging Sensations (all five senses), not just the sense the question targets. Key insight: the facilitatee's images run faster and are infinitely more dimensional than the facilitator's questions — the questions are merely signposts pointing the way. Real example from class: actress re-experiencing accidentally running into an ex-husband she still loved — reexperienced many original emotions and sensations. Post-facilitation: actors feel they know more about each other and have shared a meaningful personal experience — this sets up the next level of involvement.

imaging_sensations.name: Imaging Sensations
imaging_sensations.definition: An alternate imaging approach in which each of the five senses functions as a full, equal imager — without the question-and-response framework of conventional Sense Memory — allowing all senses to respond simultaneously as emphasis shifts organically from one to another.
imaging_sensations.details: Contrasted with Sensory-and-Imaging combination: instead of asking sensorial questions, the senses function as autonomous 'imagers' with their own 'brain.' Each sense is given equal emphasis — overcoming the culturally conditioned dominance of the visual sense. Best used when the question-and-response process would be intrusive: sleep-wake state work, Free-Association Imaging, unconscious programming. Analogy: conventional sensory approach = Phillips screwdriver; Imaging Sensations = flat-blade screwdriver — both needed for different jobs.

sources.summary: Imaging Sensations: senses function simultaneously as equal imagers without the question-response structure of conventional Sense Memory.
To train Imaging Sensations, initially over-emphasize non-visual senses to counteract conditioned visual dominance.
Used when sensorial questioning would be intrusive: sleep-wake states, free association, unconscious programming.

imaging_with_sense_memory.name: Imaging with Sense Memory
imaging_with_sense_memory.definition: The practice of combining imaging (creating an inner vision of a place or event) with systematic sensorial questions directed at each sense individually, making the image a richer, more tangible, multi-dimensional reality.
imaging_with_sense_memory.details: The actor begins by 'sketching in' the overall image visually, then asks sensorial questions answered specifically in the relevant sense organ. Unlike pure Imaging Sensations, the senses are engaged one at a time through questions, then combined. This approach solidifies and dimensionalizes the image; especially useful for Affective Memory work. Morris distinguishes it from pure Sense Memory: in combined imaging+SM, while emphasis is on one sense, all five respond simultaneously. Key principle: Sense Memory responses happen IN the sense organ, not intellectually.

sources.summary: Imaging with Sense Memory: sketch in overall image, then ask sensorial questions responded to in specific sense organs.
Difference from pure Sense Memory: in combined imaging+SM, while emphasis is in one sense, all five senses respond simultaneously.
The combination creates greater conscious and unconscious reality than either approach alone.

additions.content: Applied to relationship work in 'night, Mother: Actress playing Jessie talks to the actress playing Thelma but images her own real mother using Imaging Sensations; the other actress images her own daughter in relation to the first. The technique blends living reality with the hypothetical reality of the play until the latter becomes acceptable as truth. Both actresses are simultaneously creating the other character in the image while actually responding to the real actor's behavior. With experience, this produces a cumulative unconscious absorption of both realities.

sense_memory.name: Sense Memory
sense_memory.definition: A foundational acting technique for memorizing the impact of stimuli on the five senses — recreating real sensory experiences by directing attention to and responding within each specific sense organ.
sense_memory.details: Responses happen in the sense organ itself — not intellectually or verbally described. Forms the basis of much of Morris's system; explored in depth in No Acting Please. Used as a choice approach and as part of imaging. Distinguished from Imaging Sensations: SM focuses on one sense at a time through questions; Imaging Sensations allows all senses to flow simultaneously.

sources.summary: Sense Memory is a process for memorizing stimulus impact on senses; the response happens in the specific sense, not intellectually.

relaxation_cluster.name: Relaxation Cluster
relaxation_cluster.definition: The group of preparatory exercises that constitute the actor's first step in the work session: a relaxation technique followed by Sensitizing and Personal Inventory.
relaxation_cluster.details: Purpose: release tension (which covers real emotional life), elevate sensory accessibility, contact moment-to-moment feelings, freely express impulses. Tension is the actor's primary enemy — it blocks authentic emotional life. Only after achieving the Relaxation Cluster state can the actor address craft obligations organically.

sources.summary: Relaxation Cluster: relaxation technique + Sensitizing + Personal Inventory; the first preparatory step before any craft work.

mind_body_connection.name: Mind-Body Connection
mind_body_connection.definition: The documented phenomenon that mental activity — including images — directly affects physical bodily functions, demonstrated by medical imaging research showing visualization increased white blood cell counts.
mind_body_connection.details: Morris cites: 20 subjects who visualized making more white blood cells twice daily for 6 weeks significantly increased their count. Quantum Healing (Chopra) referenced: natural intelligence of body, receptor cells respond to imaging. New field: psychoneuroimmunology. For actors: the same mind-body power means imaging can produce genuine physiological and emotional responses.

sources.summary: University experiment: 20 subjects who imaged making white blood cells increased their count significantly.
Quantum Healing: body's natural intelligence responds to imaging; psychoneuroimmunology as emerging field.

unconscious_programming.name: Unconscious Programming
unconscious_programming.definition: The deliberate use of repeated imaging to imprint experiences, characters, relationships, and emotional patterns into the unconscious so they organically inform the actor's performance from below conscious control.
unconscious_programming.details: By repeatedly imaging the life of a character — their relationships, environments, history, inner life — the actor builds a parallel unconscious background for the character. Results manifest in the actor's dreams and waking behavior over time. Unlike intellectual analysis, this process gives the character's reality to the unconscious and lets it do the work. A full chapter (Chapter 8) is devoted to the structured process of programming the unconscious.

sources.summary: Imaging can communicate with and program the unconscious — repeated images sink in and ventilate through dreams and conscious behavior.
The process of programming the unconscious should be structured: image the people, places, relationships, hopes, and inner life of the character.
Repeated imaging creates the character's unconscious background — influences dreams and waking behavior.

selective_emphasis.name: Selective Emphasis
selective_emphasis.definition: A technique used in conjunction with imaging where the actor focuses attention on and amplifies only the parts of a choice or experience that are emotionally meaningful or relevant to the obligation, while de-emphasizing the rest.
selective_emphasis.details: Used when only part of a choice is impelling — the actor does not recreate the whole experience but zeros in on the meaningful component. Example: if an obligation requires grief, and the choice is a complex event, the actor selectively emphasizes the grief-producing moments.

sources.summary: Selective Emphasis: used with imaging when only part of a choice is meaningful — actor amplifies that part, de-emphasizes the rest.

additions.note: In Programming the Unconscious, Selective Emphasis is the craft technique used to isolate only the life experiences that parallel the character's psychological structure—deliberately excluding all experiences that produced balance or contradicting traits. Morris's 'John Jones' example: he selectively emphasized experiences of abuse, discrimination, humiliation, and being chased home from school, while excluding the balancing love and support that actually made him healthy. This selective isolation of parallel experiences is how the actor's life becomes a mirror of the character's—not by virtue of similarity, but by deliberate creative manipulation.

the_unconscious.name: The Unconscious
the_unconscious.definition: The vast reservoir of stored experience, emotion, impulse, and psychic material that exists beneath conscious awareness and that contains approximately 95% of the actor's true talent.
the_unconscious.details: The unconscious speaks a different language from consciousness — images, symbols, not words. C.G. Jung: 'Images are the language of the unconscious.' Stores all past experiences in its own organizational system; conscious work with imaging, dreams, and sensory exploration creates a conduit to it. Reaching the unconscious is the actor's ultimate goal; acting from the unconscious produces unforgettable performances.

sources.summary: Jung epigraph: 'Images are the language of the unconscious.'
95% of the actor's talent lives in the unconscious — accessing it is the primary goal.

additions.note: Images are the language of the unconscious (Chapter 6 framing). Jung's collective unconscious: all human knowledge of all ages may be stored there. Strasberg: 95% of an actor's talent is in the unconscious. Two obstacles to unconscious access: (1) the actor's need to know what's coming next; (2) fear of losing control. Solution: preparation, eliminating fears/dependencies, encouraging impulsive expression. Multiple techniques for establishing communication: Personal Inventory, Nonverbal Personal Inventory, Primal Moan, Primitive Abandonment, Rebirthing, Fears Exercise, Externals/Animals, Imaging.

fantasy_imaging.name: Fantasy Imaging
fantasy_imaging.definition: A form of imaging involving outrageous, improbable, or wish-fulfillment scenarios — going beyond realistic daydreaming into imagined events and experiences the actor has not had.
fantasy_imaging.details: Done for pleasure, wish fulfillment, aggrandizement, excitement, and adventure. As an acting tool: can be used to elevate the imagination and as a choice to produce specific emotional states. Morris gives the example of the Oscar-acceptance fantasy — a full sensory-rich imaging event that produces genuine emotional experience. Even fantasy images engage all five senses and produce real physiological responses.

sources.summary: Fantasy Imaging involves improbable, wish-fulfillment scenarios; used for pleasure and as an acting tool.
Oscar acceptance fantasy example: detailed multi-sensory imaging of an imagined event produces real emotional experience.

two_person_verbalized_imaging.name: Two-Person Verbalized Imaging
two_person_verbalized_imaging.definition: A form of Verbalized Imaging practiced by two actors simultaneously or in alternation, where each verbalizes their imaging process so the partner hears it, enabling mutual influence, discovery, and relationship depth.
two_person_verbalized_imaging.details: Can be done: (a) simultaneously — each verbalizes their own separate choice, not necessarily sharing images; (b) one at a time — the listening actor responds to partner's imaging, paralleling elements or imagining specifically what the other is creating. The second approach builds deeper mutual involvement and teaches each actor what affects the other. Reveals the partner through what is explicitly said AND through the unspoken spaces between words.

sources.summary: Two-Person Verbalized Imaging: two actors verbalize imaging simultaneously or in turns; builds mutual involvement and teaches what affects the partner.

simultaneous_guided_imaging.name: Simultaneous Guided Imaging
simultaneous_guided_imaging.definition: A sub-variant of Guided Imaging in which both actors simultaneously facilitate each other — each is both facilitator and facilitatee, responding to the other's questions with their own respective choices.
simultaneous_guided_imaging.details: Both actors alternate asking sensorial questions; both respond by imaging their own choices. If actor A asks 'What do I hear?', both A and B respond within their own choice. Both actors must know the ultimate goal and creatively manipulate the facilitation toward the desired emotional area. Major difference from Guided Imaging: each actor facilitates himself (out loud) while the other hears and responds.

sources.summary: Simultaneous Guided Imaging: both actors facilitate each other simultaneously; each responds to the other's questions with their own choice images.

shared_imaging.name: Shared Imaging
shared_imaging.definition: A technique in which one actor verbally shares vivid images of a personal experience or event while the other actor images the same events — building shared history, mutual understanding, and relational depth between scene partners.
shared_imaging.details: The describing actor images vividly as he shares; the listening actor tries to sensorially imagine the same things. Key difference from Simultaneous Guided Imaging: the listening actor does NOT substitute his own parallel choice — he specifically images what the partner is describing. Builds a feeling of having actually shared experiences in real life. Can be done scene-by-scene through a play to create complete extra-textual history. In hypnagogic state, these shared images may slip into dreams and deepen the unconscious bond between actors. Five uses: (1) involvement technique; (2) familiarity with partner's life; (3) building shared history; (4) mutually creating environments and events of the play; (5) unconscious programming potential.

sources.summary: Shared Imaging: one actor shares images aloud; other images the same events — builds shared history.
Five uses of Shared Imaging listed; can be used scene-by-scene through a play; hypnagogic-state shared imaging may enter dreams.
Shared Imaging example: actress shares cemetery visit images for bittersweet mother obligation; partner images exactly what she describes.

mental_imaging.name: Mental Imaging
mental_imaging.definition: The most natural and common form of imaging — purely internal visualizations in the mind's eye, without sensorial engagement through the body — which Morris considers insufficient for acting but acceptable as a starting point.
mental_imaging.details: Mental imaging is what we do automatically from birth: seeing pictures in the mind's eye, hearing with 'ears inside the brain.' It does stimulate sensory responses and affects behavior and bodily functions — it works for healing and self-improvement. BUT: for acting, it falls short because it stays in the head and doesn't create impelling reality. The difference: mentally imaging a room on fire vs. actually creating the smoke and flames sensorially. Morris does NOT include mental imaging as one of the seven recommended imaging techniques, but acknowledges it as a natural starting point that should be transcended.

sources.summary: Mental imaging is the most natural form but stays in the head — insufficient for acting's demand for impelling reality.
Mental imaging stimulates responses but an actor must create actual sensory reality (smoke and flames), not just think about it.

instrumental_imaging.name: Instrumental Imaging
instrumental_imaging.definition: The application of imaging techniques specifically to free the actor's instrument — eliminating obstacles (tension, fear, self-consciousness, low self-esteem) through visualization rather than through behavioral exercises.
instrumental_imaging.details: Works by programming the unconscious with new images of the actor as free, confident, powerful, and expressive. More powerful than standard instrumental exercises because it reaches the unconscious and can create lasting change rather than temporary Band-Aid effects. Used daily as part of an actor's work schedule, not just before performing. Four specific sub-areas: Imaging Physical Relaxation, Imaging Environments, Relaxation-through-Involvement Imagery, Instrumental Therapy Imaging.

sources.summary: Instrumental Imaging addresses obstacles through visualization; reaches the unconscious for lasting change.
Daily imaging in problem areas progressively builds new self-image; more powerful than behavioral exercises alone.

imaging_physical_relaxation.name: Imaging Physical Relaxation
imaging_physical_relaxation.definition: An instrumental imaging technique in which the actor visualizes and tactilely images (via Imaging Sensations) his own body as physically relaxed, comfortable, and free — promoting actual relaxation as the body responds to the image.
imaging_physical_relaxation.details: Done sitting or lying down, eyes closed then opened. Actor images going through his entire body, seeing and feeling looseness and well-being. Can also image himself relaxed in tense circumstances (auditions, romantic encounters, meetings with intimidating people) — with repetition, the image becomes the reality. Addresses not just present tension but long-term physical habits and posture.

sources.summary: Imaging Physical Relaxation: image yourself loose and relaxed through entire body; use tactile Imaging Sensations; image yourself relaxed in stressful circumstances to build new behavioral pattern.

imaging_environments.name: Imaging Environments
imaging_environments.definition: An instrumental imaging technique in which the actor creates a beautiful, relaxing, or inspiring environment through Story Imaging or Fragmented Imaging, using it to achieve a state of relaxation, involvement, and well-being.
imaging_environments.details: Place can be real or imagined — whatever has previously affected the actor with relaxation and well-being. Morris prefers natural environments (ocean, forest, desert, sunsets). Process: begin with olfactory or another non-visual sense, then expand to all senses. Can also image being buoyant in water (the Shimadi tank experience) or rocking (rocking chair, carousel, being rocked by a parent). Used at the beginning of class, before rehearsal, or before performance.

sources.summary: Imaging Environments: create relaxing real or fantasy place; all senses; ocean/nature examples.
Ocean environment example; rocking/floating imagery variants.

instrumental_therapy_imaging.name: Instrumental Therapy Imaging
instrumental_therapy_imaging.definition: The use of daily imaging to address and progressively eliminate specific acting obstacles — fears, self-consciousness, shyness, low self-esteem, emotional blocks — by reprogramming the unconscious with new, antidotal self-images.
instrumental_therapy_imaging.details: Addresses what standard acting exercises address but with longer-lasting results because it targets the unconscious. Process: identify the specific obstacle → image the antithetical behavior daily → the unconscious processes and begins feeding back new patterns to conscious behavior. NLP context: fears originate as images/thoughts; changing those images can eliminate the fear. Two-step approach to fear elimination: (1) identify and fully re-experience the fear source through imaging; (2) change the image — rewrite the outcome, add strength, change the response. On-the-spot technique: Fragmented Imaging of benevolently accepting audience/environment when suffering from fear of performance.

sources.summary: After relaxation and Personal Inventory, actor identifies specific obstacles and selects imaging antidotes.
Daily imaging in obstacle areas progressively builds new self-image; works by programming the unconscious.
Imaging is more powerful than standard exercises because it solicits the genius of the unconscious for permanent change.
Common obstacles listed: tension, fears, shyness, self-consciousness, lack of self-esteem, social conditioning, guilt.

additions.content: Specific obstacle areas with imaging antidotes: (1) Shyness — Fantasy Story Image of speaking to thousands with passion and thunderous response; start small (one person) and work up. (2) Self-consciousness — Story Image of approaching material with confidence; or Fragmented Imaging of positive responses to work. (3) Low self-esteem — Story Image building from appreciation of self to involving others; include specific physical, mental, and verbal strengths. (4) Self-involvement — create images that excite curiosity and selfless involvement; image the world as an endlessly interesting place. (5) Fear of expressing antisocial emotions (anger, rage) — Story Image of righteously confronting the original source of the conditioning (e.g., an overbearing parent) and expressing full anger; rewrite the outcome to one of resolution and understanding. (6) Conflict phobia — Fantasy Story Image of being powerful, articulate, and unintimidated in conflict; progress to genuine encounters. (7) Social conditioning/obligation — any of the above techniques applied to specific behavioral restrictions. Key principles: (a) belief + disciplined repetition are the two ingredients; (b) imaging before sleep or in sleep-wake state gets into unconscious more rapidly; (c) imaging an obstacle in reverse plants antidotal images in same unconscious structures that created the problem. Morris notes he can no longer distinguish some long-practiced images from actual memories — the technique works to that degree.

fear_elimination_imaging.name: Fear Elimination through Imaging
fear_elimination_imaging.definition: A two-step imaging technique for permanently eliminating acting fears: first fully re-experiencing the fear's origin through sensory imaging, then systematically changing the image to a successful, empowered outcome.
fear_elimination_imaging.details: Step 1: Identify the fear, isolate its origin (childhood or later experiences). Re-create those experiences through imaging with all five senses, allowing the full original emotional response. Repeat multiple times until clear. Step 2: Change the image — alter the outcome so that the actor is powerful, articulate, confident; replaces the fear-image with a success-image. The unconscious, which learned the fear through images, can unlearn it the same way. Richard Bandler (NLP): 'the brain can take one instantaneous experience to learn something thoroughly for life' — the same mechanism can be reversed. On-the-spot version: use Fragmented Imaging to image the audience as supportive and accepting; sneak from real-available benevolent signals into imaging beyond what exists.

sources.summary: Two-step fear elimination: re-create fear's origin in full sensory imaging, then change the image to empowered outcome.
On-the-spot fear technique: acknowledge the fear aloud (or silently), then use Fragmented Imaging to see accepting, supportive faces everywhere.

personal_inventory.name: Personal Inventory
personal_inventory.definition: An exercise in which the actor checks in with himself moment-to-moment to find out exactly where he is emotionally, physically, and psychically — the honest self-assessment that precedes any craft work.
personal_inventory.details: Done after relaxation to discover what obstacles remain. Involves asking: 'What am I feeling right now? What is my physical state? What emotional life is available to me?' The starting point for determining which instrumental or craft step comes next. Part of the Relaxation Cluster.

sources.summary: After relaxing, actor does Personal Inventory to find out where he is and what obstacles exist before proceeding.

additions.note: Personal Inventory is the very first step in establishing communication with the unconscious (Chapter 6). As the actor does it daily, some responses begin to feel deeper, more abstract, mysterious—evidence of unconscious material surfacing. Subliminal images also arise; the actor should acknowledge and respond to all of them. Personal Point of View extends Personal Inventory by adding 'How do I feel about X?' in relation to specific objects, people, and thoughts.

imaging_as_way_of_life.name: Imaging as a Way of Life
imaging_as_way_of_life.definition: The principle that imaging should not be a technique only used in class or rehearsal, but a continuous daily practice woven into every aspect of the actor's life — from waking to sleeping.
imaging_as_way_of_life.details: Actor is an actor 24 hours a day, not just when working. Everything must be of interest; constant curiosity and engagement with the world. Working with imaging during sleep — manipulating and participating in dreams. Imaging should become so habitual that it sharpens the senses, stretches the imagination, and opens new areas of self-knowledge continuously. Daily journaling of imaging experiences adds to a growing self-knowledge document.

sources.summary: Acting is 24-hour commitment; imaging should be part of the actor's daily life schedule, not just in class.
Every time you image spontaneously, become conscious of it; this builds the habit of structured imaging.

fantasy_imaging_as_craft.name: Fantasy Imaging (as Acting Tool)
fantasy_imaging_as_craft.definition: The use of elaborate imagined scenarios — not based in real experience — as choice approaches for scene obligations, instrumental preparations, or to elevate the imagination and liberate the actor from depression or blocks.
fantasy_imaging_as_craft.details: Like Believability, connects to the child in the actor — hooks into play. Most fun to do; treats acting as a game. Combined with other imaging techniques: Story, Fragmented, Free-Association. Specific uses: (1) to address depression and elevate emotional state before performance; (2) to fill information gaps about a character's backstory; (3) to elevate imagination when no real experience fits the obligation. Example: actor depressed before a show uses clown fantasy — a fully sensory-rich Story Image — to transform his state to joy and expansiveness.

sources.summary: Fantasy Imaging: probably the most fun; like playing a game; used to elevate imagination and as choice approach.
Fantasy can address depression before a performance by selecting a pleasurably memorable topic.
Clown fantasy example: full sensory Story Image of being a clown transforms depression into elation and readiness to act.

i_am_five_years_old_imaging.name: I'm Five Years Old (Imaging Variant)
i_am_five_years_old_imaging.definition: An instrumental imaging exercise in which the actor begins with the prompt 'I'm five years old and I...' and allows spontaneous childhood images to arise and flow through multiple ages — eliminating spot obstacles while elevating affectability and unconscious involvement simultaneously.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging.details: Unlike the conventional verbal version, this variant uses pure imaging: the actor responds to the prompt with images, not words. Each prompt ('I'm five years old and I...') generates a specific image — the kindergarten classroom, the bedroom at night, shadows on the wall, etc. The actor can stay at one age for a while, then progress to six, seven, eight, or beyond. Triple benefit: (1) selfless involvement takes actor off the spot; (2) varied childhood images elevate affectability in many directions simultaneously; (3) fragments of unconscious memories crash into consciousness, dimensionalizing the BEING state. Used as an on-the-spot instrumental exercise before addressing craft obligations.

sources.summary: I'm Five Years Old imaging variant: use the prompt but respond with images not words; progress through ages; triple benefit: involvement, affectability, unconscious access.
Stay at each age until productive responses cease; complete when involved, affected, and off the spot.

object_imaging.name: Object Imaging
object_imaging.definition: An imagination-elevating exercise in which the actor selects an object in the room, looks at it, and creates an elaborate sensory-rich story or set of images about its history, personality, relationships, and inner life — using the object as a springboard for imaginative exploration.
object_imaging.details: Two variants: (1) Story Object Imaging: create a complete story about where the object was before, what environments it inhabited, what it would say, what its parents looked like, how it relates to other objects in the room. (2) Impulsive Object Imaging: look at the object and impulsively allow whatever image arises — no logic or story required; images can be completely nonsensical. The goal is NOT coherence but an active, unleashed imagination. All images must engage all five senses. Purpose: stretch and elevate the imagination; when logic drops away and impulsive/nonsensical images arise, the imagination is beginning to hook into the unconscious.

sources.summary: Object Imaging: select a room object; create its story (history, personality, relationships, voice); engage all five senses.
Impulsive Object Imaging: look at object and allow any impulsive image to arise without logic; bizarre images indicate active imagination.

relationship_imaging.name: Relationship Imaging
relationship_imaging.definition: An imaging technique in which the actor silently begins to modify the real behavior of the other actor in front of him — changing what is actually happening into a variety of imagined relationship dynamics — creating new emotional realities from available stimuli.
relationship_imaging.details: Done simultaneously and silently — the other actor need not know. Start with what is real between you; then begin to image modifications (e.g., the other actor flirting with you, being angry, showing affection, etc.). Both actors' responses to each other affect each other in real time, creating a self-reinforcing dynamic. Promotes the beginnings of a relationship that can be built upon over time. Applicable even with actors meeting for the first time.

sources.summary: Relationship Imaging: silently image modifications in the other actor's real behavior; both actors' responses affect each other; promotes relationship beginnings.
Both actors can do this simultaneously without knowing what the other is doing; their responses affect each other in real time.

parallel_imaging.name: Parallel Imaging
parallel_imaging.definition: An imaging practice in which the actor creates images of himself living the life of the character — experiencing her experiences, relationships, conflicts, and history — using specific circumstances of the play while also weaving in real personal parallels.
parallel_imaging.details: Done individually (not with a partner), outside of rehearsal. The actor images himself in the center of the character's circumstances — not as observer but as participant. Real personal parallels are woven in wherever they resemble the events of the play, blending reality with hypothesis. Cumulative effect: hypothesis becomes reality through absorption; supports unconscious programming. Specifically suggested by Morris in preparation for 'night, Mother: actress images herself having the life of the character, including events not depicted in the play.

sources.summary: Parallel Imaging: image oneself having the character's life, experiences, and relationships; use personal parallels to blend reality with the hypothetical.

craft_preparations.name: Craft Preparations
craft_preparations.definition: The second major category of preparation (after instrumental preparation), in which the actor uses imaging and other techniques to address specific obligations of the material — creating the realities of time and place, relationship, emotional life, and character.
craft_preparations.details: Follows instrumental preparation; actor must first be free, open, and accessible. Like instrumental work, craft preparations are categorized by Morris in two groups: (1) Relationship and Relating preparations (2) Obligation-specific preparations (time and place, emotional, character, historic, thematic, subtextual). The actor must take a complete inventory after instrumental preparation to determine exactly what craft preparation is needed.

sources.summary: Craft preparations: the second major preparation category; follows instrumental work; addresses specific material obligations.

time_and_place_obligation.name: Time and Place Obligation
time_and_place_obligation.definition: The first and most foundational acting obligation — the requirement that the actor genuinely experience the environment described by the material (location, time of day, season, weather, historical period) as if he were actually there.
time_and_place_obligation.details: Often preempts other obligations and should be addressed first unless another obligation is more urgently compelling. The character's behavior is influenced by the environment on both obvious and subtle levels; the actor's relationship to the place affects and elicits unconscious emotional points of view. The actor must select a place from his own life that will mean to him what the play's environment means to the character. Selection should NOT be made hastily; the place must carry historical, emotional, and behavioral resonance. Imaging is an excellent approach for creating time and place — using either Imaging Sensations or Imaging with Sense Memory depending on vividness/remoteness of the choice.

sources.summary: Time and Place: first major obligation; stimulates unconscious emotional points of view; actor must select personal parallel that carries the same meaning.
Selection of the place must consider character history in relation to it, its present meaning, and its behavioral impact in the scene.

elevating_the_imagination.name: Elevating the Imagination
elevating_the_imagination.definition: The deliberate cultivation and expansion of the actor's imaginative capacity through daily imaging practices — restoring the innovative, playful, sensory-rich imagination of childhood that adult social conditioning has suppressed.
elevating_the_imagination.details: Imagination is necessary but not sufficient — it must be active, innovative, and creative enough to supply genuine impulsion. Adult social conditioning suppresses imagination; actors must actively work against this. Four main techniques for elevating the imagination through imaging: (1) Object Imaging; (2) Impulsive Object Imaging; (3) Fantasy Imaging; (4) Free-Association Imaging. All four should engage all five senses and be practiced daily as part of the actor's work schedule. When images become bizarre or illogical, this indicates that the imagination is hooking into the unconscious — a positive sign.

sources.summary: Imagination is mandatory but must be developed — most adults are far less imaginative than they were as children.
Four imaging techniques for elevating imagination: Object Imaging, Impulsive Object Imaging, Fantasy Imaging, Free-Association Imaging.

believability.name: Believability
believability.definition: The actor's cultivated willingness and ability to believe in imaginary circumstances as if they were real — the capacity to make hypothetical realities feel true to the instrument so that genuine impulses and emotions result.
believability.details: Like imaging, hooks into the child in the actor — connects to the sense of play and pretend. Imaging is one of the most powerful tools for building believability — repeated imaging of circumstances eventually makes them feel real. Cumulative imaging + Believability: as the actor repeatedly images the play's realities, these are absorbed into the unconscious and the willingness to believe grows organically. Believability is not a decision ('I will believe this') but an accumulated sensory-emotional state built through process.

sources.summary: Imaging in combination with Believability cumulatively absorbs realities into the unconscious and supports willingness to believe in the material.
Imaging takes actor beyond imagination alone to total realization — being in the place; requires elevated gullibility and willingness to believe.

additions.note: Believability is the process of combining a little truth or reality with a great deal of untruth or fantasy. Used in conjunction with Imaging as craft technique: when an actor images their choice saying or doing things that did not really happen but are amazingly close to the real relationship, the mix of real and imagined memories merges so that the distinction fades. This is directly useful in character background imaging (Bluebeard example: actor was really poor and felt unwanted, then extended those realities into fantasy abuse episodes).

available_stimulus.name: Available Stimulus
available_stimulus.definition: The technique of using what is actually present in the environment — real objects, the actual behavior of the other actor, genuinely existing elements — as stimuli and building blocks for imaging, before extending into images beyond what really exists.
available_stimulus.details: Foundational principle in Morris's system: always start from what is real before extending into what is imagined. In on-the-spot fear work: find real evidence of benevolence in the actual environment first; then image beyond it. In relationship imaging: begin with the real behavior of the other actor; then begin to modify it in imagination. Available stimulus is what the actor has; imaging is how he extends and transforms it.

sources.summary: On-the-spot fear work: find real benevolence in environment first; then extend through imaging beyond what exists.
Relationship Imaging starts with what is real between the actors and then modifies in image.

additions.note: In the Waiting for Lefty example, Available Stimulus exploration between actors Shaun and Priscilla preceded all other choice work. By looking for real feelings between themselves and selectively emphasizing critical glances or moments of levity as evidence of rejection or indifference, they established an authentic involvement. Available Stimulus exploration also helps identify similarities between the scene partner and a personal choice before Endowments.

emotional_obligation.name: Emotional Obligation
emotional_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations an actor must fulfill; requires the actor to experience specific emotions demanded by the scene or script. The actor selects a choice (music, experience, or person) that personally stimulates those emotions and uses Imaging as the choice approach to create them.
emotional_obligation.details: Three primary choice types for the emotional obligation: (1) Creating a Piece of Music – selecting evocative music and using Imaging to hear it internally while performing; (2) Creating an Experience – selecting a personal past event that produced the needed emotions and using Imaging to re-enter that experience; (3) Creating a Person – selecting a real or imagined person who evokes the needed feelings and using Imaging to conjure them. Example 1: union organizer scene – actor uses 'Big Country' theme music imaged internally to feel heroic grandeur. Example 2: 'Hearts' play – actress about to fall uses imaging of real motorcycle accident to access terror. Example 3: 'Dear Jane' letter scene – actor uses two-part imaging: Fragmented Imaging for love feelings about Tim, then Fantasy Story Imaging to see Jane receiving the letter.

emotional_transitions.name: Emotional Transitions
emotional_transitions.definition: The technique of moving between emotional states during a scene by using Fragmented Imaging in real time as the story demands emotional shifts.
emotional_transitions.details: When a scene requires the actor to move from one emotional state to another (e.g., joyful anticipation → grief and shock), Fragmented Imaging is used transitionally. Example from Chapter 4: an actor anticipating a friend's arrival uses positive Fragmented Images (pug nose, red hair, laughter, sparkle in eyes). When the friend enters and something is wrong, the actor immediately shifts to contrasting Fragmented Images (sad eyes, stooped shoulders, gray skin, fear and pain). The images occur rapidly with no words, purely as sensory flashes that produce the new emotional state organically.

character_obligation.name: Character Obligation
character_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor must create who the character is – their psychological profile, intelligence, emotional life, physical rhythms, and behavioral patterns – and assimilate those qualities into their own persona.
character_obligation.details: Process: (1) Compare and contrast self with character physically, emotionally, behaviorally. (2) Create a background life for the character using Imaging – events and experiences prior to the play's action. (3) Incorporate parallel realities from the actor's own life mixed with fantasy (Believability). Any imaging technique can be used; Story Imaging works especially well for building complex character backgrounds. Key insight: choices need not be literal – the actor does not have to experience exactly what the character experiences, only what produces equivalent impulses. Character background imaging can tap the actor's unconscious when real experiences are woven in.

character_background_imaging.name: Character Background Imaging
character_background_imaging.definition: The use of Imaging techniques—especially Story Imaging—to construct an imaginary biographical history for a character prior to the play's first scene, creating episodes and experiences that explain who the character is and why.
character_background_imaging.details: The actor reads the script thoroughly, absorbs all author-supplied facts, notes what other characters say about this character, and constructs the character's life in chronological episodes. Two options: (1) Total fantasy – entirely imagined events consistent with the character profile. (2) Blended approach – mixing real parallels from the actor's life with fantasy (Believability), so that real and imagined memories eventually merge and become indistinguishable, enriching the actor's unconscious engagement. Example 1 (Story Imaging / Bluebeard): Morris builds childhood abuse, humiliation, and rejection episodes for a serial killer character in Love from a Stranger. Example 2 (Fantasy Story Imaging / Starbuck): An actor playing the con-man Starbuck in The Rainmaker creates a parallel fantasy (e.g., his own Hamlet fantasy) that matches Starbuck's grandiose, belief-in-his-own-lies energy. Example 3 (I'm Five Years Old + Fantasy Imaging): For a shy, phobic character, the actor uses 'I'm five years old and I…' to unlock childhood imagery and then immediately moves into fantasy consistent with the character's history.

historical_obligation.name: Historical Obligation
historical_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: when the play is set in a specific historical period, the actor must absorb and re-create the customs, mores, morality, religion, politics, science, and daily life of that era.
historical_obligation.details: Any of the seven imaging techniques can be used to create the realities of the historical period. The actor images the sights, sounds, smells, and textures of the time and place to inhabit the world of the play authentically.

thematic_obligation.name: Thematic Obligation
thematic_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the actor must understand and embody the author's statement or message in the play, selecting choices that generate the emotions and behavior that manifest the thematic content.
thematic_obligation.details: When the character carries the thematic statement, the actor uses Imaging to create the inner life that makes that statement real and organic.

subtextual_obligation.name: Subtextual Obligation
subtextual_obligation.definition: One of the seven obligations: the underlying emotional quality or essence running through the entire play that must permeate every choice the actor makes.
subtextual_obligation.details: Examples: a feeling of hopelessness throughout, or conversely a quality of hope and vitality. Every choice must accommodate this underlying quality. Imaging can be used to sustain the subtextual emotional state across scenes.

imaging_as_craft_technique.name: Imaging as Craft Technique
imaging_as_craft_technique.definition: The use of Imaging as the practical process (approach technique) that supplies the method for making another choice approach work when that approach does not have its own built-in technique.
imaging_as_craft_technique.details: A craft technique is a choice approach used in tandem with another. Choice approaches that require a craft technique include: Endowments, Objects That Come into Contact with the Body, Externals, Affective Memory, Essences and Abstracts, Illusions and Restrictions, Personal Realities, Believability, Prior Knowledge, Subpersonalities, and Selective Enhancement. Imaging (along with Sense Memory and Sensory Suggestion) can serve as the craft technique for all of them. Example: If the choice approach is Objects That Come into Contact with the Body (e.g., a tuxedo to create formality), Sense Memory or Imaging Sensations can be the craft technique that actually creates the felt reality of wearing the tuxedo. Imaging as craft technique with Affective Memory: Story Imaging + a combination of Sense Memory and Imaging Sensations used to chronologically re-create the original experience and trigger unconscious reliving. Imaging as craft technique with Endowments: Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations used to sculpt a chosen person's features, voice, smell, and essence onto the scene partner.

affective_memory_imaging.name: Affective Memory via Imaging
affective_memory_imaging.definition: Using Imaging (Story Imaging + Imaging Sensations) as the craft technique to trigger and relive an emotionally impactful past event, accessing unconscious responses that reproduce the original emotions.
affective_memory_imaging.details: Process: Start with conventional sensory questions (Sense Memory approach) to build the environment chronologically, responding with each sense. As the actor moves deeper, Imaging Sensations gradually replaces the question-and-answer format, and all five senses activate simultaneously. The actor proceeds chronologically through every detail leading up to the emotional peak. Repeated practice shortens the process from hours to minutes. Success depends on: sensory craftmanship, sufficient memory of the event, patience and specificity, not omitting critical elements. Morris's example: re-creating his traumatic first TV acting job (The Court of Last Resort) with a chaotic foreign director and humiliating gun mishaps on set. Application in rehearsal: Waiting for Lefty (Odets) – actors Shaun and Priscilla used Affective Memory with Imaging as craft technique, plus Endowments and Believability, to build the five-year intimacy of Joe and Edna's relationship.

additions.note: Affective Memory can be used as an unconscious preparation—not just as a choice for addressing material obligations. Select a very meaningful experience loaded with unconscious potential (deep history, important relationships, strong emotional content). The goal is to prime unconscious life for general use. If addressing material: give yourself plenty of time; start before the impact of the experience and proceed chronologically. All sensory questions must be in the here and now. Be prepared to fail: if unsuccessful, go over the components again more specifically.

conglomerate_endowment.name: Conglomerate Endowment
conglomerate_endowment.definition: The state in which a scene partner and the actor's imaginary choice merge into a single composite presence, so that the actor responds to the real actor as if everything emanating from them came from the chosen person.
conglomerate_endowment.details: Achieved through sustained Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations applied to the scene partner. The actor compares available stimuli (real similarities between the partner and the choice), then images the choice's unique features onto the partner. The two become a conglomerate: while the actor receives a strong sense of the choice person, they must still respond to what the partner actually does and says. The real behavior of the partner becomes, in the actor's experience, the behavior of the choice person.

choices_need_not_be_literal.name: Choices Need Not Be Literal
choices_need_not_be_literal.definition: The acting principle that a choice used to fulfill an obligation need not replicate the literal circumstances of the script; it only needs to produce equivalent emotional impulses in the actor.
choices_need_not_be_literal.details: Example from Chapter 4: If a character faces death, the actor's choice is not necessarily physical death – it could be any ending or loss that produces the same emotional impact. The choice that works is the one that stimulates the right feelings. This principle underlies Believability (mixing truth and fantasy) and the entire choice-approach system: what matters is the actor's authentic organic response, not surface correspondence to the text.

subpersonalities.name: Subpersonalities
subpersonalities.definition: Distinct internal energy patterns or 'selves' within a person, each with its own drives, behaviors, and worldview. Originally theorized by C.G. Jung as archetypes of the collective unconscious, elaborated by Dr. Hal Stone and Dr. Sidra Stone into the 'Voice Dialogue' system.
subpersonalities.details: Primary selves: created to protect the Vulnerable Child by gaining parental and social approval. Examples: Protector, Controller, Pleaser, Pusher, Inner Critic, Perfectionist, Achiever, Workaholic, Moralist (Martin Luther). Disowned selves: opposite energies suppressed because they were punished or disapproved of. Examples: Magical Child, Mischievous Child, Vulnerable Child, Warrior, Killer, Aphrodite/Don Juan, Satanic energies, Beach Bum, Dictator. Disowned selves often surface in dreams. Aware Ego: the goal of Voice Dialogue work – a conscious center that can recognize, hold, and balance all selves without being dominated by any one. In acting: Subpersonalities is Choice Approach #21, a MEGAPPROACH. Morris developed a four-part Imaging approach to elicit subpersonalities for acting.

additions.note: Subpersonalities is Choice Approach #21 and a MEGAPPROACH. Morris uses ten approach techniques. Every subpersonality physically inhabits a different space in the room; moving between spaces is essential to the work. Each self has a distinctive physical appearance even within the imaging process (e.g., magical child vs. vulnerable child; protector looking like General Patton). The disowned selves are the hardest to access and require most experience. Imaging can be incorporated into all ten approach techniques.

voice_dialogue.name: Voice Dialogue
voice_dialogue.definition: A therapeutic and developmental technique created by Dr. Hal Stone and Dr. Sidra Stone in which a facilitator speaks directly with different subpersonalities, asking each 'self' to move to a separate physical space and speak for itself, thereby making unconscious selves conscious.
voice_dialogue.details: The facilitator asks to speak with a subpart; the subject physically moves to a new area of the room, inhabiting that energy. The facilitator then dialogues with that self, gathering information about how it functions, what it wants, and how it influences the subject's life. Over time, the subject develops an Aware Ego that can recognize and balance all selves. Morris uses Voice Dialogue as a basis for his four-part Imaging approach to subpersonalities in acting.

aware_ego.name: Aware Ego
aware_ego.definition: The goal of Voice Dialogue and subpersonalities work: a conscious center of identity that recognizes all of one's selves, sits between them, and can consciously raise or lower any self's energy without being dominated by any single subpart.
aware_ego.details: With an aware ego, the individual is no longer unconsciously run by primary selves. They can choose to embrace all of their selves and relate to each objectively, creating balance. For actors, cultivating an aware ego means having access to a wider range of emotional and behavioral energies.

subpersonalities_as_acting_tool.name: Subpersonalities as Acting Tool (Choice Approach #21 / MEGAPPROACH)
subpersonalities_as_acting_tool.definition: Using subpersonalities as a choice approach: the actor identifies which subpersonality matches the character's dominant energy, accesses that self through one of ten techniques, and allows it to carry the character's sense of life, emotional life, relationships, and behavior through the entire piece.
subpersonalities_as_acting_tool.details: Once the actor is inhabited by the correct subpersonality, it knows exactly how to respond to other characters without intellectual direction. It can address the emotional obligation, character obligation, relationship obligation, and sense of life simultaneously – in one fell swoop. It is a MEGAPPROACH because it could constitute an entire acting system by itself. Two modes of use: (1) The subpersonality IS the choice – selected because it matches the character's dominant energy (e.g., Citizen Kane → Obsessive Achiever + Dictator). (2) The subpersonality is a preparation – accessed to feel the right emotional state before tackling the material (e.g., Hero self to combat fear). Characters in dramatic literature: - Kane (Citizen Kane): Obsessive Achiever + Dictator. - Eddie/May (Fool for Love): Don Juan + Aphrodite (obsessive). - Jessie ('night, Mother): Victim → Responsible Self. - Othello: Warrior → Victim → Killer. - Romeo: Romantic Child ↔ Warrior. - Felix (Odd Couple): Pusher/Perfectionist + Responsible Self + Judgmental Self. - Gordon Gecko (Wall Street): Frightened Child → Master Controller + Achiever + Perfectionist + Workaholic. - Salieri (Amadeus): Abandoned Child + Warrior/Satanic.

imaging_subpersonalities.name: Imaging to Access Subpersonalities (Morris's Four-Part Approach)
imaging_subpersonalities.definition: Morris's specific four-step Imaging technique for eliciting subpersonalities: (1) Create the subpart through imaging; (2) Step into its energy field and become it; (3) Look in a mirror to verify and deepen the embodiment; (4) Image through the subpersonality's eyes – relating to the environment and eventually to material obligations.
imaging_subpersonalities.details: Step 1 – Create the image: Relax and choose a physical space where the sub's energy feels located. Begin imaging what that subpersonality looks like, using all five senses. Do not impose a concept – image intuitively. Build sensory detail: height, hair, skin, laughter, eyes, dress, physical structure. Each subpart looks distinctive: the magical child differs from the vulnerable child; the protector may resemble General Patton in military uniform. Step 2 – Step into the energy: When the subpart is tangible, move into its exact space and allow yourself to inhabit that energy – like putting on an overcoat. Encourage your entire being to become that energy. Step 3 – Mirror work: Look into a mirror, observe physical changes, find the subpart's presence in your face and eyes. Stay until you see and feel the transformation. Step 4 – Image through the subpersonality: Relate to the environment from that self's perspective (the same environment looks completely different). Then encourage the subpart to image imaginary objects, people, and experiences – supplementing its emotional impetus. Finally, move gently toward the material's character obligations. Process duration: 8-10 minutes minimum. Any imaging technique can be used. Accessibility: Primary selves are most accessible; opposite selves are further back; disowned selves require the most experience to coax forward.

ten_subpersonality_approach_techniques.name: Ten Approach Techniques to Access Subpersonalities
ten_subpersonality_approach_techniques.definition: The ten methods Morris uses to access and elicit subpersonalities, ranging from interpersonal facilitation to solo practice:
ten_subpersonality_approach_techniques.details: 1. Voice Dialogue: Two-person facilitation; facilitator speaks directly with each self as the subject physically moves to different spaces. 2. Role-playing: Solo; actor in aware-ego state asks a subpart to emerge and speak in an uninterrupted monologue. 3. Journal: Written dialogue between the actor's 'I' and a named subpart; useful when speaking aloud is impossible. 4. Tape Recorder: Facilitator records questions, leaves silences, then actor records responses as the subpart. 5. Movement and/or Dance: For nonverbal subparts; explore a range of movements and rhythms to discover which elicit which energy. 6. Drawing and Sculpture: Stream-of-consciousness drawing or clay sculpting can pique unfamiliar selves. 7. Using Choices: Creating objects, people, places, or experiences as choices that inadvertently (or deliberately) access a subpersonality. 8. Music: Different music elicits different selves; catalogue music by the subpart it activates. 9. Externals: Animals, people, insects, inanimate objects can open doors to subparts – especially disowned and primal selves. 10. Imaging: Morris's four-part approach (see imaging_subpersonalities). Note: All ten can incorporate Imaging to some degree.

subpersonality_archetypes.name: Subpersonality Archetypes (Full List)
subpersonality_archetypes.definition: The universal archetypes (present in all people) plus common individual subparts that Morris and the Stones have identified.
subpersonality_archetypes.details: PRIMARY SELVES (most common): Protector, Controller, Pusher, Inner Critic, Pleaser, Responsible Self, Achiever, Workaholic, Perfectionist, Conciliator, Moralist/Martin Luther, Good Father, Bad Father, Good Mother, Bad Mother, Hero, Wise Person/Wisdom Voice, Obedient Son/Daughter, Judgmental Self, Master Controller. CHILD ARCHETYPES: Vulnerable Child, Frightened Child, Magical Child, Mischievous Child, Lonely Child, Rebellious Child, Obedient Child, Shy Child. SEXUAL/SENSUAL: Aphrodite (female sexual energy), Don Juan (male sexual energy). DISOWNED/OPPOSITE SELVES (examples): Warrior, Killer, Dictator, Satanic/Demonic, Beach Bum, Rebellious Son/Daughter, Needy Ones, Abandoned Child, Victim. WITNESS STATE: a nonverbal self not attached to the outcome; observes without communicating with other selves; different from Aware Ego.

witness_state.name: Witness State
witness_state.definition: A specific subpersonality – a nonverbal self that is not attached to the outcome of a voice-dialogue session or the dynamics between selves. It witnesses what happens and takes it in, but is not involved in communicating with any subparts.
witness_state.details: Different from the Aware Ego: the Aware Ego is actively involved in communication and can control the energy of selves; the Witness State simply observes. Used at the end of voice-dialogue sessions when the facilitator recaps observations.

ultimate_consciousness.name: Ultimate Consciousness
ultimate_consciousness.definition: The pinnacle acting experience: a state in which the actor's conscious and unconscious merge completely, producing transcendent, ineffable performance in which both actor and audience are transported to another dimension.
ultimate_consciousness.details: Lee Strasberg said ninety-five percent of an actor's talent is in the unconscious. Ultimate Consciousness occurs when unconscious connection is deep enough to take over. It is not consistently achievable and is unpredictable, but it is the pinnacle of unconscious achievement. The closer consciousness and the unconscious are to each other, the deeper, fuller, and more dimensional is all acting work – even short of the Ultimate Consciousness peak. Detailed in Morris's book Acting from the Ultimate Consciousness (p.42).

personal_point_of_view.name: Personal Point of View
personal_point_of_view.definition: An exercise related to Personal Inventory: the actor continuously asks 'How do I feel about…?' in relation to all objects, people, and thoughts in the environment, building self-awareness and stimulating unconscious responses.
personal_point_of_view.details: Done after or as part of Personal Inventory, or totally separately. Explores the actor's subjective emotional relationship to everything around and within them, including people not present, thoughts, impulses, and images that surface during the exercise. Deepens consciousness and invites unconscious material into awareness.

re_creating_unconscious_impulses.name: Re-Creating Unconscious Impulses Through Imaging
re_creating_unconscious_impulses.definition: The practice of deliberately re-creating an impulse or image that arose spontaneously during Personal Inventory (apparently from the unconscious) using Imaging techniques, in order to establish a conscious relationship with that unconscious material.
re_creating_unconscious_impulses.details: Rationale: each time you re-create what seems to be an unconscious impulse, you bring it to the surface again—forcing your open hand into a small hole in the ground, making the hole larger each time. Method: start anywhere in the experience; use any imaging technique (Story, Fragmented, Verbalized, etc.); allow images to run themselves out; do not edit impulses. Example: a poker beside the fireplace triggers a flash of violent childhood memories. The actor uses Fragmented Imaging to re-create: bricks, smoke, father's red face, mother moving back, feeling hot then cold. Important: do not try to reproduce the exact emotional response from the first time; allow whatever comes (e.g., sadness instead of anger) – the unconscious will supply its own authentic response.

nonverbal_personal_inventory.name: Nonverbal Personal Inventory
nonverbal_personal_inventory.definition: A variant of Personal Inventory in which the actor responds to 'How do I feel?' entirely in sounds rather than words, allowing impulses and images to surface without verbal mediation.
nonverbal_personal_inventory.details: Words can impede impulses and block the liberation of unconscious images. Sound-based response bypasses the verbal filter and directly accesses deeper emotional and unconscious material. The actor is aware of all thoughts, feelings, impulses, and images that arise during the exercise. Recommended frequency: daily, or several times a week.

primal_moan.name: Primal Moan
