primal_moan.definition: A primal exercise in which the actor assumes the fetal position on the floor and produces a deep, progressively louder moan from the lower abdomen, crashing through emotional blocks and soliciting unconscious impulses.
primal_moan.details: Process: Curl into fetal position (hands palms-together between thighs). Begin moaning softly from deep in the lower abdomen. Gradually build louder and deeper. The sound should vibrate from the lower body—not get trapped in the throat. The moan eventually embodies a range of emotions from vulnerability to rage. Often produces uncontrollable crying; allow all emotions to run their full course. Images arise during the process: these are acknowledged as unconscious communications when used to explore the unconscious (rather than for emotional release alone). Suggested images during the moan can steer the unconscious into specific memory areas. With practice, becomes a reliable primer for unconscious participation in acting work.

primitive_abandonment.name: Primitive Abandonment
primitive_abandonment.definition: A standing primal exercise in which the actor makes large, animal-like sounds and movements with total abandonment, building toward a rhythmical, prehistoric energy that solicits unconscious impulses, primal emotions, and disowned subpersonalities.
primitive_abandonment.details: Unlike the conventional Abandonment exercise, this is done standing. Starts with large physical and vocal commitment; becomes more rhythmical and animal over time. Sounds: howling, grunting, guttural hurt/angry animal sounds. Body has total freedom of movement. Effects: liberation of angry/hostile impulses, emancipation of sexual energy, piquing of satanic/demonic subpersonalities, primal images including unfamiliar faces (possibly unconscious/prenatal in origin). Can also produce unexpected emotional shifts (e.g., aggressiveness → benevolence as images of great size and omnipotence arise). After completion: allow quiet assimilation and meditative state before moving to acting work. Primary purpose: conditioning the instrument to communicate with the unconscious. When used as preparation: transition directly into a choice that addresses the material.

birth_experience_exercise.name: Creating the Experience of Birth (Rebirthing Exercise)
birth_experience_exercise.definition: A physical exercise in which the actor simulates moving through the birth canal, keeping eyes closed, to appeal to unconscious prenatal memories and activate deep primal responses.
birth_experience_exercise.details: Based on rebirthing therapy (belief that birth trauma affects people throughout their lives). The actor physically maneuvers through a simulated tunnel/birth canal. Eyes remain closed until emergence. Upon opening: see everything as if for the first time; do not attach labels. Expected responses: fear, anxiety, feeling of being out of control, wonderment, disorientation. Emotional responses are evidence of success. Images surfacing during the exercise are likely from unconscious/prenatal sources. Do not attempt to understand or interpret images—just allow and express.

fears_exercise.name: Fears Exercise
fears_exercise.definition: An exercise in which the actor lists and articulates all their fears (conscious and partially unconscious), then either images each fear in full sensory detail or incubates each fear quietly, to access unconscious material rooted in those fears.
fears_exercise.details: Two approaches: (1) Image the fear: 'I'm afraid of dying' → image yourself expiring or on a respirator with all five senses. The image produces impacting unconscious responses. (2) Incubate the fear: stay with it, let it run its course, find out what it means to you personally. Do not image – just allow the fear to exist. Fears are often denied and pushed into unconscious recesses; exploring them offers a bonanza of unconscious material. Nightmares are the unconscious's most vivid expression of fear. Exercise can be repeated often; yields new fears each time.

dream_work.name: Working with Dreams
dream_work.definition: The practice of recording, re-creating, and exploring dreams as a primary technique for establishing communication with the unconscious and accessing deep emotional material for acting.
dream_work.details: Practical start: Keep a notepad or tape recorder by the bedside; record everything immediately upon awakening (dreams fade quickly). Approach: Do not analyze or interpret the dream. Instead, go back over the experiences emotionally. Re-creating the dream through Imaging: treat it like any other experience—use all five senses, start from the beginning, allow images to come up spontaneously. Best done lying down, eyes closed, after a relaxation preparation. Unconscious fears are most vividly expressed in nightmares. Recurring dreams are particularly significant: they indicate persistent unconscious material seeking attention. Morris's recurring dream example: driving into a lake, associated with childhood near-drowning in a Chicago pool at age 12. Dreams in the service of acting: re-creating an impacting dream as a preparation primes unconscious sources to support the emotional obligations of a scene. Technique: Close eyes, relax fully (sleeping state = relaxed), begin imaging the dream from the beginning. Use any imaging technique. Allow experiences to unfold rather than leading them.

disconnected_impulsivity.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity.definition: An impulsivity exercise in which the actor expresses impulses, images, words, and associations at high speed with no premeditation, no logical connection required between responses, and no commentary between impulses—as a technique for piquing unconscious material.
disconnected_impulsivity.details: Originally used to antidote concept and leadership (intellectual control) in acting. Morris discovered it could also stimulate unconscious responses: some impulses that arise feel unconnected to anything conscious and seem to come from an unconscious trough. One-person version: actor starts with current environment, expresses impulses verbally/vocally/silently as they arise. All responses are in image form. Two-person version: a director/facilitator provides a single suggestion ('Grade-school classroom') and calls out 'Switch!' at intervals to force the next image. Director may also inject specific suggestions to steer toward particular memories. Duration: up to 10 minutes. After completion: actor feels adrenaline, impulses bottlenecking, emotions converging. Adding Meaningful Suggestions variant: after noting impactful items from the first exercise, do a second run using those specific items as pre-loaded images to deepen unconscious connection. Morris's example: 'Fillmore Street' → father → Northwestern → Boy Scouts, each triggering distinct and powerful emotional states.

switch_trick_exercise.name: Switch-Trick Exercise
switch_trick_exercise.definition: A variant of Disconnected Impulsivity in which a director or teacher rapidly switches an actor from stimulus to stimulus with very few words ('Switch!'), pushing them to the razor's edge of impulsivity and often releasing congested emotional life from the unconscious.
switch_trick_exercise.details: Two-person structure: director provides an initial suggestion; actor responds with any image. Director calls 'Switch!' and actor must produce the next image immediately. Director may interject his own suggestions at any point. All responses are images (not concepts or verbal explanations). Effect: surprise/shock value keeps the actor off balance; prevents premeditation; releases emotions that have been relegated to the unconscious. After 10 minutes: actor experiences highly stimulated state, converging emotions, unconscious life brought closer to consciousness.

hypnagogic_state.name: Hypnagogic State
hypnagogic_state.definition: The transitional state between waking and sleep in which the unconscious liberates images into consciousness. Characterized by involuntary, startling images that seem unrelated to conscious thought—flashes of the unconscious while still partially awake.
hypnagogic_state.details: In the hypnagogic state, images often startle the person back to full waking. The experience usually lasts only seconds. The goal in Morris's system is to prolong the state and consciously work with the images that arise. Logy Exercise: the primary technique for deliberately inducing the hypnagogic state. The actor lies prone, exaggerates body weight to slothlike heaviness, makes a sound using the word 'logy,' and encourages themselves toward the sleep threshold. When they feel themselves beginning to drop off, they encourage images and remain conscious of them. With repetition they can 'work' the hypnagogic state. Risk: falling asleep. Application: as a preparation before rehearsal to enlist unconscious cooperation.

additions.note: In the programming context: the actor prepares a specific image in advance (e.g., being trapped and helpless for Tom in Glass Menagerie) and plants it at the precise hypnagogic moment. Images can be literal (bound in a small room) or abstract/symbolic (inside a glass cylinder filling with water). Fragments of parallel experiences also work. With practice, the window can be elongated. Deprogramming can also use this state to implant antidotal images.

daydreaming_technique.name: Daydreaming as Conscious Practice
daydreaming_technique.definition: The deliberate and conscious practice of daydreaming as a technique for seducing the unconscious, elevating imagination, and creating a preparation state for acting.
daydreaming_technique.details: Instead of daydreaming less than consciously, the actor sets aside scheduled time for it and allows the daydream to run its course impulsively. Unexpected impulses that steer the daydream into unfamiliar directions are to be encouraged—these may come from unconscious places. Once unconscious cooperation is enlisted through daydreaming, it continues to flow into conscious behavior, enriching it with unpredictable dimension and depth. Used as a general unconscious preparation before beginning to approach a scene. Morris's example: daydreaming about winning the lottery led to deep images of personal values, family, and eventually to strange unfamiliar environments and inner voices asking what's important—evidence of unconscious intrusion. The next chapter (Programming the Unconscious) explores daydreaming further.

night_games.name: Night Games
night_games.definition: Childhood-derived bedtime fantasy journeys—full sensory stories told to oneself before sleep—practiced consciously as an adult to elicit unconscious life, elevate imagination, and prepare for acting work.
night_games.details: Morris used night games as a child before knowing anything about acting or imaging. He would create elaborate fantasy stories with all senses engaged, speaking, crying, and acting through each one. He believes this is where his imaging journey started. Adult practice: use Story Imaging, Fragmented Imaging, or any imaging technique to create fantasy journeys at bedtime. Effect: elevates imagination, hacks a path for the unconscious to follow, influences dreams, and liberates deep choices for acting work.

unconscious_suggestions_cluster.name: Unconscious Suggestions (Exercise Cluster)
unconscious_suggestions_cluster.definition: A cluster of imaging exercises in which the actor systematically re-creates specific categories of formative environments from their life in order to liberate unconscious impulses associated with those memories.
unconscious_suggestions_cluster.details: Categories (also called 'areas'): 1. Bedrooms of your life – re-create every bedroom from childhood through adulthood:    walls, floor, ceiling, objects, toys, stuffed animals, parents entering,    smells of the pillow, bedsheets, dreams remembered. 2. Schoolrooms of your life – re-create every classroom chronologically:    teacher's face and voice, other children, smell of chalk/glue, the school clock,    the schoolyard. Allow images from other schoolrooms to arise spontaneously. 3. Religious experiences of your life – houses of worship, parochial school,    nuns/priests/ministers, prayers, uniforms, confession, incense, choir. 4. Playgrounds of your life – special places where you played and created fantasies:    physical environment, companions, pretend games, emotional atmosphere. Techniques: any imaging technique can be used; Story Imaging for specific environments, Fragmented Imaging for the sensory texture, Free-Association Imaging for a potpourri. Workshop application: after spending an hour imaging all suggested places, actors perform monologues with remarkable depth, dimension, and inspiration. Key rule: Do NOT comment, acknowledge, or evaluate impulses—just express them.

additions.note: Additional categories of Unconscious Suggestions (continuing from earlier listing): 5. Toys of your life – specific childhood toys as available stimuli and images:    names, colors, textures, smells of glue, feelings associated with each. 6. Joys of your life – graduations, awards, victories, falling in love, first curtain call,    first film job; use Imaging Sensations for each event. 7. Romances, relationships, sexuality – chronologically image each significant person:    use Free-Association Imaging, starting with the name and face. Allow images to blend. 8. Abstract places from childhood – unidentifiable fragments, scary shapes,    unfamiliar sensory flashes; approach with Logy as preparation, then suggest an age. 9. Fragments of memories – no chronology; clear the brain and allow fragments from    any period of life to arise spontaneously (Fragmented/Disconnected approach). 10. Pieces of dreams and nightmares – single images from dreams; re-create them. 11. Experiences where you can't distinguish dreams from reality – approach without     trying to determine truth; just create images and allow unconscious response. Additional suggestions (brief list): funerals, blocked events, early childhood traumas, early sexual images.

inner_voices.name: Inner Voices and Images
inner_voices.definition: Spontaneous mental voices and images that arise during the day with no apparent cause—identified by Morris as messages from the unconscious that, when attended to, constitute a waking connection with the unconscious.
inner_voices.details: Most people dismiss inner voices as stray thoughts. Morris teaches actors to acknowledge them, follow them, and express their responses. Sources include: unconscious material piqued by choices being explored, subliminal environmental stimuli, unresolved emotional material. A PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCER includes all moment-to-moment inner voices and images in their behavior through the lines of a scene, so that the audience discovers the next moment at the same time as the actor. Listening practice: pay attention to any inner voice, no matter how fleeting; note names spoken, things said, abstract fragments. With practice, waking communication with the unconscious becomes more reliable and frequent.

professional_experiencer.name: Professional Experiencer
professional_experiencer.definition: Morris's term for an actor who has developed the ability to honor all moment-to-moment impulses, inner voices, and images as they arise—including those from the unconscious—and to include them organically in performance.
professional_experiencer.details: A Professional Experiencer does not premeditate responses. They allow the next moment to emerge from a collision of conscious choices and unconscious impulses. The audience discovers what is happening at the same moment the actor does. This requires: impulsive expression, no preplanning of reactions, trust in the unconscious, and the craft to follow any impulse through the written material.

additions.note: The deprogramming mantra explicitly invokes this concept: 'I am a professional experiencer and a creative artist. My motives were positive.' The professional experiencer always knows the difference between character reality and self reality.

perspective_inventory.name: Perspective Inventory
perspective_inventory.definition: A progressive weekly exercise for recovering early childhood memories: starting by reviewing yesterday, expanding each week to a broader time range, progressing backward through your life until you reach earliest memories—progressively loosening experiences stored in the crevasses of the mind.
perspective_inventory.details: Week 1: Review today's events. Week 2: Recall events of the previous week. Week 3: Go back over the entire month. Week 4: Go back over the year (focus on memorable events). Week 5+: Go back in increments of years. Each remembered experience brings surrounding ones with it. Stay with a period for several weeks before going further back. Questions after each event: What happened before and after it? Who was there? These pique other memories from that period. Effect: like drilling for oil—boring through layers of rock until reaching a gushing well. Some actors claim to remember their birth or before using this technique. Often combined with 'I'm five years old and I…' for accessing specific early ages.

dream_journal.name: Dream Journal
dream_journal.definition: A mandatory record of all dreams, kept by recording every morning immediately upon awakening—both complete dreams and fragments—to retain unconscious material that would otherwise fade, and to build a repository of choices for acting.
dream_journal.details: Practical requirements: keep a pad and pencil or tape recorder by the bedside. Record as soon as possible after waking (dreams fade extremely quickly). Record: complete dreams, fragments, symbols, emotional tone. Even incomplete dreams are valuable—fragments may contain key symbols. Some people dream in episodes across consecutive nights—all parts must be recorded. The journal serves multiple purposes: 1. Memory preservation (dreams fade back into the unconscious within minutes). 2. Symbol identification (symbols become clearer over multiple dreams). 3. Building a repertoire of choices for acting. 4. Detecting patterns (recurring themes, characters, scenarios). After recording: you may write interpretations or notes on emotional responses, but trust feelings and instincts about the dream's meaning rather than overthinking.

additions.note: The Dream Journal has a four-section structure for each dream entry: 1. Describe the dream completely (all parts, feelings, comments). 2. Interpret: identify symbols, recognize people (subjective vs. objective),    note feelings and reactions, use active imagination. 3. Translate into acting components: identify choices, list potential    obligations the dream could address. 4. Re-create:    A. From original perspective (as dreamt).    B. From a different perspective (assume the identity of another dream figure).    C. Objectively (as observer, removed from participation). After accumulating many dreams: the actor has a large repertoire of choices across a wide variety of obligation types.

dream_symbols.name: Dream Symbols
dream_symbols.definition: Images, objects, animals, or actions in dreams that carry symbolic meaning beyond their literal appearance, representing aspects of the self, relationships, or unconscious messages.
dream_symbols.details: Types: - Subjective symbols: represent parts of the self. - Objective symbols: represent other people or external situations. Common symbols and their general meanings: - Large body of water / drowning: the unconscious; material wanting to surface. - Automobile: ego; outer behavior and direction in life. Speeding = need to slow down;    accident = ego attitude leading in the wrong direction. - Snake: wisdom, instinctive energy (both frightening and fascinating). - Animals in general: domestic = civilized self; wild = instincts/unconscious;    fish/dolphins = contents from deep inside wanting to surface;    dogs = relationship, warmth, personal touch. - Flying: need to escape OR potential to soar to heights. - Being naked in public: helplessness, feeling exposed, stripped of protections. - People who look different from who they 'are': the unconscious's use of substitutes. Note: symbols must always be interpreted in context of what is happening in the dreamer's waking life at the time.

active_imagination.name: Active Imagination (Jung)
active_imagination.definition: A technique created by C.G. Jung for consciously relating to the unconscious: the dreamer places a dream figure in a chair, begins a dialogue with them, or expresses dream content through writing, movement, or drawing—as a way of receiving answers from unconscious material.
active_imagination.details: Three approaches: (1) Dialogue: Place a dream figure in a chair facing you. Ask: Who are you?     What do you want? Why are you behaving this way? Allow the figure to answer.     Answers may be startling and come from the unconscious. (2) Stream-of-consciousness writing: Write impulses and responses to the dream     as they arise. (3) Movement: Reinvest in the dream and encourage your body to move, expressing     the dream's energy physically. Combination with Imaging: Add sensorial elements while relating to the dream figure—create them through Imaging Sensations, building a richer and more tangible reality. The more tangible the figure, the better the chance of accessing unconscious material. Active Imagination is different from Imaging but completely compatible with it. Note: Similar techniques used in subpersonality work (voice dialogue).

dreams_as_choices.name: Dreams as Choices
dreams_as_choices.definition: Using a dream (or cluster of dreams with a common theme) as the choice for addressing acting obligations—particularly for psychologically complex characters whose inner life comes from deeply unconscious forces.
dreams_as_choices.details: When to use dreams as choices: - When obligations are intricate and dimensional enough to require unconscious depth. - When the character's psychology is clearly rooted in unconscious forces   (suppression, inner demons, deep trauma, complex ambivalence). - When the character seems to be 'beyond the actor's emotional range' on a conscious level. Characters particularly suited to dream choices: Hamlet, Willy Loman, Kane (Citizen Kane), Tom (The Glass Menagerie narrator), Macbeth, Lady Macbeth, Dr. Jekyll/Mr. Hyde, the Preacher in Rain, The Three Faces of Eve. Process: identify the theme or emotional quality needed; look for a dream that contains that theme; re-create the dream through Imaging as preparation. If you cannot find the right dream: order one up through pre-sleep suggestion. Dreams can address: character, emotional, relationship, historical, thematic, subtextual obligations; time and place; mood and ambience. They can also elicit disowned subpersonalities that cannot be accessed otherwise.

dream_ordering.name: Dream Ordering
dream_ordering.definition: The practice of consciously requesting or 'ordering' a specific dream by thinking about the desired theme repeatedly during the day and giving yourself a direct pre-sleep suggestion—in order to receive unconscious material relevant to a specific acting challenge.
dream_ordering.details: Process: Identify the emotional, relational, or psychological area you need to explore. Think about it throughout the day. Just before sleep, state clearly: 'I would like to have a dream concerning [specific issue/character/relationship].' With practice, you may receive a dream that addresses the request. This is discussed more fully in the next chapter (Programming the Unconscious). Residual bonus of regular dream work: even when not deliberately ordering dreams, unconscious impulses continue to flow into conscious behavior during waking hours.

programming_the_unconscious.name: Programming the Unconscious
programming_the_unconscious.definition: A structured process by which the actor repeatedly images character-parallel experiences from their own life—before, during, and after sleep—so that the unconscious accepts those imaged events as if they had actually happened and begins to spontaneously support the character's reality in performance.
programming_the_unconscious.details: Core principle: IF YOU CONTINUE TO IMAGE AN EVENT AND EXPERIENCE IT OVER AND OVER, THE UNCONSCIOUS WILL ACCEPT IT AS IF IT HAD ACTUALLY HAPPENED. The actor consciously images, but the unconscious provides the inspiration and dimension. Prerequisites: - Psychological stability; ability to distinguish real from imagined. - Well-developed eleventh level of consciousness. - Only for major, multifaceted roles—not small, underdeveloped parts. SIX-STEP PROCESS: 1. Read the play (objective reading → character analysis → mini-journal). 2. Create all pre-play experiences: image events before the play begins and between scenes. 3. Image all character relationships using personal parallels. 4. Address the written scenes, creating imaging parallels for each. 5. Create a series of images of yourself successfully moving through all character experiences. 6. Keep a programming journal throughout. Additional tools within the process: - Walter Mitty Option (fantasy when no real parallel exists). - Hypnagogic/pre-hypnagogic states for deeper unconscious imprinting. - Dreams: encourage dreams related to character; use Symbolic Imaging of dream symbols   as triggers in rehearsal and performance. - Dream Journal recording all dreams throughout the character work. The goal: the actor's unconscious participates organically in the character's reality, producing impulses, dimension, and inspiration that transcend conscious technique.

additions.note: Step 5: Image yourself playing the part—stand outside yourself and see yourself as the character in every environment; Story Imaging works best. Step 6: The Programming Journal—records all parallels, imaging techniques, comments, and dream entries; enables both continuation of the process and eventual deprogramming. Additional programming options: endow the other actor with the face/qualities of the parallel person; allow imaging responses to feed directly into scene behavior; use Selective Emphasis and Creative Manipulation to adjust imperfect parallels; work scenes in sequence to build natural behavioral evolution.

walter_mitty_option.name: Walter Mitty Option
walter_mitty_option.definition: A fail-safe alternative within the Programming the Unconscious process: when the actor cannot find a real parallel for a scene's circumstances, they create a totally imaginary experience using Imaging—repeating it so many times that the unconscious eventually accepts the imaginary event as real.
walter_mitty_option.details: Named after James Thurber's Walter Mitty, who lived almost entirely in vivid fantasy exploits his real life couldn't afford him. Application: image all of the actions and emotional life that occur in the scene as if they had really happened to you. Center yourself in the action repeatedly. With the help of the unconscious and repetition, you will eventually confuse reality and fantasy—which is the goal. Two modes: (1) Supplement: use real parallels whenever possible, Walter Mitty option when not. (2) Total approach: apply Walter Mitty option to the entire programming process     if it works better for the circumstances. Note: this is the same principle as Believability but applied systematically across an entire character preparation process.

additions.note: Detailed application in programming: actor can do an entire play imaging himself having all the character's experiences (thought, feeling, behavior) purely in fantasy. Three options for other characters: actual co-actors, real people from your life, or entirely imagined faces. Connects to child-energy and makes the process pleasurable (a major asset). Fantasy and truth become intermingled and indistinguishable with repetition.

eleventh_level_of_consciousness.name: Eleventh Level of Consciousness
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.definition: Morris's term for the state in which the actor is fully and simultaneously aware of their character's reality AND the actual performance environment (cues, other actors, blocking, audience)—a dual consciousness that allows organic character work without losing craft awareness.
eleventh_level_of_consciousness.details: Prerequisite for Programming the Unconscious: the actor must be sufficiently developed as a craftsman to maintain this dual awareness. Without it, the actor risks being unable to distinguish imagined reality from performance reality—a psychological danger when programming the unconscious for powerful or extreme roles. Also referenced in Morris's book Acting from the Ultimate Consciousness.

additions.note: Detailed: the actor who is fully involved on levels 1-10 retains a meta-awareness on level 11 that cannot interfere with involvement. Develops over time with craft. Beginners may only operate at level 2-3 (self-conscious). As craft grows, level pushes back. Even during ultimate-consciousness experiences, level 11 must exist or it becomes a psychotic episode. Representational actors do not need it; experiential actors require it to trust the emotional process safely. Mantra: 'DON'T LEAVE HOME WITHOUT IT!'

programming_journal.name: Programming Journal
programming_journal.definition: A comprehensive written record kept throughout the Programming the Unconscious process, capturing all imaging experiences, parallel choices, dream content, symbol discoveries, and character insights for a specific role.
programming_journal.details: Started at the very beginning of the process (Step 6 of the six-step outline). Contents: - Mini-journal of character analysis from script reading. - Notes on character similarities and differences from the actor's life. - List of parallel people for each character relationship. - List of parallel places and environments. - All imaging experiences and their emotional results. - All dreams recorded during character work (even those not obviously related). - Notes on dream symbols and their potential as performance triggers. - Character journals (written thoughts/experiences about each relationship). The journal creates a repertoire of choices that the actor can return to at any time during rehearsal and performance.

imaging_yourself_playing_the_part.name: Imaging Yourself Playing the Part (Step 5)
imaging_yourself_playing_the_part.definition: One of the six programming steps: standing outside yourself and imaging yourself as the character moving through all environments of the play with total organic reality and full involvement.
imaging_yourself_playing_the_part.details: The actor visualizes himself as the character inhabiting every environment and experience in the play, observing from outside himself, as an athlete images making a perfect play. Can be done at the start to psych up, or after laying the foundation of parallel real-life experiences. Story Imaging works best for this step. The goal is to see oneself succeeding organically in the character.

deprogramming_the_unconscious.name: Deprogramming the Unconscious
deprogramming_the_unconscious.definition: The deliberate process of reversing the programming journey after completing a role, restoring the actor's authentic unconscious reality by objectifying what was real versus what was created.
deprogramming_the_unconscious.details: Deprogramming begins at the first reading of the play—before any programming—by establishing conscious awareness of what is real. The journal record makes it possible. The five-step process: (1) Establish clarity about the purpose from the outset; (2) Maintain the eleventh level of consciousness throughout; (3) Commit to being affected while staying grounded; (4) Use a mantra repeated many times (e.g., 'I am not Tom; I am very different; my relationships are healthier'); (5) Work through each journal entry in order, re-imaging true reality to overwrite the parallels. The actor objectifies every parallel image by contrasting it with actual reality ('My real mother doesn't wash dishes; we have an automatic dishwasher...'). Reprogramming antidotal images into the hypnagogic state and encouraging counter-dreams also helps. The body's homeostatic function supports the process—the unconscious naturally seeks to return to organic reality.

homeostasis_of_unconscious.name: Homeostasis of the Unconscious
homeostasis_of_unconscious.definition: The unconscious's natural self-correcting intelligence that causes it to return to organic reality after programming, just as the body returns to physical equilibrium.
homeostasis_of_unconscious.details: Morris borrows the biological term homeostasis to describe how the unconscious automatically adjusts back to authentic reality after the rehearsal period ends. Research on REM sleep, hypnosis, and dream manipulation shows that subject participants returned to normal after experiments. The actor can trust this mechanism as a safety net during the programming process.

lucid_dreaming.name: Lucid Dreaming
lucid_dreaming.definition: A dream state in which the dreamer is consciously aware that they are dreaming without interrupting the dream, allowing some degree of conscious participation and direction.
lucid_dreaming.details: Morris defines lucid dreaming for acting purposes as awareness within the dream state that does not break the dream. The dreamer becomes an observer from within. When it occurs during a programming period, the actor can attempt to steer the dream into the environment of the play and the character's experiences. Aggressive conscious participation risks waking the dreamer. Lucid dreaming is among the closest contact points between conscious and unconscious mind. Morris encourages cultivating it but acknowledges it is unreliable. Redirecting an irrelevant lucid dream into a character scenario is its primary use for programming.

ordering_up_a_dream.name: Ordering Up a Dream
ordering_up_a_dream.definition: The deliberate practice of requesting a specific dream from the unconscious each night during the programming process by ending an imaging workout with a clear, demanding image of what the actor wants to dream about.
ordering_up_a_dream.details: After cataloguing dreams already activated during programming, the actor uses the imaging process each night to re-create a specific parallel experience or character event, then at the end orders a dream related to that content. The request must be specific and demanding. If the unconscious complies, the dream is recorded, and in subsequent sessions the actor re-creates elements of that dream to call it back up, building a cumulative unconscious-to-conscious connection over the rehearsal period.

blueprint_using_dreams_to_program.name: Blueprint for Using Dreams to Program the Unconscious
blueprint_using_dreams_to_program.definition: A seven-step systematic framework for integrating dream work with the programming process to build unconscious support for a character.
blueprint_using_dreams_to_program.details: 1. Record all dreams in a dream journal (may keep a separate character dream journal). 2. Study all information each dream contains. 3. Use dream content (symbols, events) as choices or impetus to stimulate parallels or further dreams—even isolate a repeated symbol and image it. 4. Repeatedly re-create significant dreams and use them as addenda to imaging character parallels. 5. Use the hypnagogic state to plant seeds that dreams can grow from. 6. Be aware of disowned subpersonalities appearing in dreams; if relevant to the character, access and incorporate them consciously. 7. Encourage and use lucid dreaming—if it occurs, steer it into the play's environment and the character's emotional reality.

fantasy_story_imaging_programming.name: Fantasy Story Imaging in Programming
fantasy_story_imaging_programming.definition: Using Fantasy Story Imaging within the programming process to create entire character scenarios from imagination when no exact real-life parallel exists.
fantasy_story_imaging_programming.details: The actor creates the character's environment and relationships entirely in imagination, following the written scene verbatim in imaging. May use the actual co-actors, fictitious people, or imagined faces. Fantasy and reality elements can be mixed. The Walter Mitty option is the vehicle for this. Example from The Glass Menagerie: actor images the dining room, lampshade, smells the spaghetti, hears Amanda's voice, watches Laura, experiences Tom's claustrophobia entirely in imagination. With repetition, the fantasy becomes indistinguishable from real experience.

imaging_as_choice_approach.name: Imaging as Choice Approach
imaging_as_choice_approach.description: Using the imaging process (creating a vivid, sensory-rich mental visualization of a person, place, event, or experience) as the method by which the actor makes a chosen object real, thereby stimulating authentic emotional life to fulfill a scene obligation.
imaging_as_choice_approach.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental, but imaging exercises also build sensory accessibility and imagination as instrumental benefits.
imaging_as_choice_approach.craft_use: Actor identifies an obligation, selects a choice from personal life, then creates that choice through imaging with full sensory engagement. As the image becomes real, it stimulates the desired emotional response organically.
imaging_as_choice_approach.when_to_use: When the obligation calls for an emotionally rich parallel that can be most efficiently created through visualization. Best when extended, immersive sensory engagement with the choice is needed.

sources.summary: Imaging is Choice Approach #26 in Morris's system.
Imaging is the 5th MEGAPPROACH — more powerful than most other choice approaches.

imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.name: Imaging with Sense Memory
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.description: A combined technique in which the actor begins with a mental image of a place, person, or event and enriches it by asking systematic sensorial questions directed at each sense organ, requiring the sense to respond specifically in its own domain.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.instrumental_use: Creates deeply real states — relaxation, environments of safety or beauty — that prepare the actor to access authentic feelings.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.craft_use: Creates the most dimensionally real version of a choice; especially important for Affective Memory work. Used to fulfill time-and-place, relationship, and emotional obligations.
imaging_with_sense_memory_technique.when_to_use: When maximum tangibility and dimensional reality of a choice are needed. Especially for Affective Memory, complex emotional obligations, or when establishing an entire environment as a starting condition.

sources.summary: Process: sketch image visually, ask sensorial questions responded to in specific sense organs, support images with deepening sensorial exploration.
Distinguished from pure Sense Memory: in imaging+SM combination, while one sense is emphasized, all five respond simultaneously.

imaging_sensations_technique.name: Imaging Sensations
imaging_sensations_technique.description: An alternate imaging approach where the actor allows all five senses to function simultaneously as equal imagers, each with its own processing, shifting emphasis organically from sense to sense without structured question-and-response.
imaging_sensations_technique.instrumental_use: Trains the actor to give equal weight to all senses — overcoming culturally conditioned visual dominance — deepening overall sensory accessibility.
imaging_sensations_technique.craft_use: Used when the question-response format would intrude on image flow: Free-Association Imaging, sleep-wake state work, unconscious programming sessions.
imaging_sensations_technique.when_to_use: When a smooth, uninterrupted imaging flow is needed — for unconscious programming, free association, and any imaging where analytical interruption would break the state.

sources.summary: Senses function simultaneously without questions; each sense becomes its own imager.
Used when sensorial questioning would intrude — sleep-wake work, free association, unconscious programming.

involuntary_imaging_technique.name: Involuntary Imaging (as Acting Technique)
involuntary_imaging_technique.description: The practice of becoming conscious of spontaneous images that arise during work on a choice or obligation, then deliberately re-creating those involuntary images to access the unconscious emotional material they represent.
involuntary_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Raises self-awareness; helps the actor learn how his instrument processes experience; reveals personal emotional patterns and potential choices.
involuntary_imaging_technique.craft_use: After identifying involuntary images generated during rehearsal, the actor re-creates them as a choice approach — producing richer, more dimensional emotional life than the original conscious choice, because the images come from the unconscious's translation of the obligation.
involuntary_imaging_technique.when_to_use: During rehearsal, when the actor wants to go beyond initial conscious choices and access deeper unconsciously mediated emotional material; also useful for repeated performances.

sources.summary: Involuntary Imaging used as an acting technique: become aware of the images, re-create them.
Class exercise: re-creating involuntary images produced more impacting and dimensional emotional life than original choices.
Theory: involuntary images are the unconscious's translation of the conscious choice, more deeply connected to the obligation.

symbolic_imaging_technique.name: Symbolic Imaging (as Acting Technique)
symbolic_imaging_technique.description: Collecting personal symbolic images (especially from dreams and sleep-wake states), recording them in a journal, and deliberately re-creating them to use as emotional triggers or as choice approaches for specific scene obligations.
symbolic_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens connection with the unconscious through regular engagement with its symbol-language.
symbolic_imaging_technique.craft_use: A symbolic image associated with a particular person, place, or emotional state can be re-created to instantly trigger that state — a powerful shortcut to specific feeling tones.
symbolic_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When a shortcut to a deep emotional state is desired; when the actor has identified reliable symbolic triggers from personal unconscious; when working to program the unconscious for a character.

sources.summary: Symbolic images can be collected, re-created, and used as emotional triggers — a powerful shortcut.
Keep a journal of symbolic images; cross-reference with dreams; use as choices.

fragmented_imaging_technique.name: Fragmented Imaging
fragmented_imaging_technique.description: A deliberately initiated, thematic but non-sequential stream of images that can rapidly address any obligation or instrumental issue — faster and less elaborate than Story Imaging, suited for speedy emotional transitions and on-the-spot work.
fragmented_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Used to rapidly elevate vulnerability, address ego, change emotional state, or shift from depression; especially useful on-the-spot before performance.
fragmented_imaging_technique.craft_use: Used as a choice approach when speedy transitions are needed, when little time is available for a more elaborate approach, and when addressing all seven obligations quickly. Analogous in speed to Sensory Suggestion relative to Sense Memory.
fragmented_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When material calls for speedy emotional transitions; when time is limited; when the actor needs a fast on-the-spot instrumental adjustment. Less appropriate when deep, sustained emotional work is needed.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging is to Imaging as Sensory Suggestion is to Sense Memory — faster, less elaborate.
Can address all seven obligations; involves creative direction and selective emphasis unlike Free-Association Imaging.

additions.note: Fragmented Imaging is also the primary technique for Emotional Transitions within a scene: when the emotional demand shifts, the actor rapidly generates contrasting sensory fragments that produce the new emotional state. The transition is wordless and immediate – pure sensory flashes. Also central to building character relationships (Death of a Salesman / Biff): actor uses Fragmented Imaging with Imaging Sensations across a series of selectively emphasized memory episodes spanning childhood to adulthood.

free_association_imaging_technique.name: Free-Association Imaging
free_association_imaging_technique.description: A deliberate but direction-free imaging process in which the actor sits or lies down, clears the mind, and allows images to arise without any steering or leadership — functioning as an involvement preparation and self-discovery tool.
free_association_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates self-consciousness and performance anxiety by creating selfless involvement; elevates affectability and emotional energy; yields insights into the actor's psychology and origins of obstacles.
free_association_imaging_technique.craft_use: During rehearsal, free-associating can liberate unconscious images related to the character's life, opening doors to emotional material that supplements the chosen approach. Also a preparation before addressing the first choice of a scene.
free_association_imaging_technique.when_to_use: As a daily practice for self-discovery and imagination elevation; before rehearsal or performance as an involvement preparation; during the rehearsal period while working on a specific role.

sources.summary: Free-Association Imaging is hands-off; initiated by design but then fully released without steering.
Six rewards: facile with imaging; involvement preparation; elevates affectability; self-understanding; new choices; unconscious access.

story_imaging_technique.name: Story Imaging
story_imaging_technique.description: Imaging that follows a coherent narrative — a story with beginning, middle, and end — created either with Sense Memory or Imaging Sensations, used to create complete choice experiences or stimulate specific emotional states.
story_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Used to stimulate ego, shift depression, elevate emotional state, create inspiring self-images, or prepare instrumentally when a sustained narrative is more effective than fragments.
story_imaging_technique.craft_use: Creates the character's complete background (birth to first scene); creates environments and relationships; addresses any obligation that requires a sustained, logically coherent scenario. Can also supply extra-textual life between scenes.
story_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When sustained narrative is needed; when filling in character backstory; when the actor wants to engage the child-like storytelling imagination; when the obligation requires a complete scenario rather than fragments.

sources.summary: Story Imaging has continuity — beginning, middle, end; done with either Sense Memory structure or Imaging Sensations.
Can be episodic; used to elevate emotional state, stimulate ego, create backgrounds; also works instrumentally.

guided_imaging_technique.name: Guided Imaging
guided_imaging_technique.description: A two-person imaging process in which one actor facilitates the other through an experience by asking sensorial questions and making suggestions, allowing the facilitatee to explore a choice without the bias of anticipation.
guided_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Creates a creative dependency relationship between the actors that deepens their bond and can be carried into the scene.
guided_imaging_technique.craft_use: Especially productive for scenes where characters have shared history; used to explore choices without premeditation; helps actors learn the imaging process when they can focus only on responding, not creating.
guided_imaging_technique.when_to_use: After some rehearsal time together (so each knows what affects the other); when the obligation involves intimate shared history; when the actor keeps anticipating and guiding his own choice exploration.

sources.summary: Guided Imaging: one leads, one responds with silent sensory images; facilitator must know all elements of the experience.
Best for scenes with shared character history; begin with surrounding realities, then reach impelling core.

shared_imaging_technique.name: Shared Imaging
shared_imaging_technique.description: A process in which one actor verbally shares images of a personal experience while the partner specifically images the same events — building mutual history, understanding, and relational depth that transfers directly to performance.
shared_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens the actors' relationship and creates a felt sense of shared experience — reducing the need for representational relationship-acting.
shared_imaging_technique.craft_use: Used scene-by-scene through a play to build complete shared history including extra-textual life; can be used in hypnagogic state to deepen unconscious bonding between actors.
shared_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When building a relationship between scene partners; when the characters have a long shared history not depicted in the script; when the actors need to experience the texture of each other's inner lives.

sources.summary: Shared Imaging: one shares images verbally; other images the same events specifically, not parallels.
Can be done scene-by-scene through a play; hypnagogic-state shared imaging may enter dreams.

imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.name: Imaging Physical Relaxation
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.description: Visualization of the actor's entire body as physically relaxed, combined with tactile Imaging Sensations throughout the body, to promote actual relaxation and a long-term shift in physical habits.
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.instrumental_use: Directly eliminates tension — the actor's primary obstacle. Can also build new physical habits over time by repeatedly imaging relaxed behavior in stressful situations.
imaging_physical_relaxation_technique.when_to_use: At the start of any work session when tension is present; as a daily practice for long-term physical change; before auditions or performances.

sources.summary: Image yourself relaxed through the whole body; use tactile Imaging Sensations; also image yourself relaxed in tense situations to build new patterns.

fear_elimination_imaging_technique.name: Fear Elimination through Imaging
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.description: A two-step imaging process for permanently eliminating acting fears: (1) fully re-experience the fear's origin through sensory imaging; (2) systematically change the image to an empowered, successful outcome — reprogramming the unconscious.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Permanently addresses lifelong fears — not a Band-Aid but a deep reprogramming. Works because the unconscious learned the fear through images and can unlearn it the same way.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.craft_use: N/A — purely instrumental, but the result frees the actor to use the craft organically.
fear_elimination_imaging_technique.when_to_use: As a daily practice when addressing a persistent fear; not for on-the-spot performance use (for that, use the benevolent-audience Fragmented Imaging variant).

sources.summary: Two-step: re-experience fear's origin sensorially, then change the image to empowered outcome.
On-the-spot: acknowledge fear, then Fragmented Image audience as supportive and accepting.

relationship_imaging_technique.name: Relationship Imaging
relationship_imaging_technique.description: A silent imaging technique in which the actor, while relating to the real other actor, simultaneously images modifications in the other's behavior — creating new emotional relationship realities from available stimuli without the partner's knowledge or coordination.
relationship_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Deepens the actor's involvement with and interest in the scene partner; promotes authentic moment-to-moment impulses.
relationship_imaging_technique.craft_use: Creates the specific relationship dynamic required by the material — endowing the partner with qualities, feelings, and behaviors the character would have. Can be started from the first rehearsal.
relationship_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When meeting an actor for the first time; when a relationship needs to be quickly established; when the actor needs to create specific relationship dynamics without relying on the partner's behavior alone.

sources.summary: Silent imaging of modifications in the other actor's real behavior; both respond authentically; creates the beginning of a real relationship.

parallel_imaging_technique.name: Parallel Imaging
parallel_imaging_technique.description: An individual imaging practice in which the actor creates images of himself living the character's life — using the play's actual circumstances as the scenario and weaving in personal parallels — building the unconscious background of the character through sustained immersive imaging.
parallel_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: N/A — purely a craft/unconscious programming technique.
parallel_imaging_technique.craft_use: Builds the character's complete reality — experiences, relationships, history — in the actor's unconscious through sustained repeated imaging. Personal parallels ensure the images are authentic rather than intellectual.
parallel_imaging_technique.when_to_use: During the rehearsal period; done individually at home or in private; repeated across multiple sessions to allow cumulative unconscious absorption. Especially important for plays with complex character backgrounds.

sources.summary: Image oneself having the character's life using the play's circumstances; include personal parallels; blend reality with hypothesis; builds unconscious character background.

i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.name: I'm Five Years Old (Imaging Variant)
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.description: An on-the-spot instrumental technique using the prompt 'I'm five years old and I...' to generate spontaneous childhood images that eliminate spot obstacles, elevate affectability, and pique unconscious involvement simultaneously.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates on-the-spot instrumental obstacles (tension, self-consciousness, self-involvement, fear of failure) by creating selfless involvement with childhood images that stretch the emotional state in varied directions and dimensionalize the BEING state.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.craft_use: N/A — purely instrumental, but the heightened BEING state enables craft work.
i_am_five_years_old_imaging_technique.when_to_use: When on-the-spot obstacles prevent the actor from reaching a BEING state; when standard relaxation techniques have not fully freed the instrument; before beginning craft preparations.

sources.summary: I'm Five Years Old imaging: respond to prompt with images not words; progress through ages; triple benefit.
Complete when involved, affected, and off the spot.

endowment_via_imaging.name: Endowment via Imaging
endowment_via_imaging.description: Using imaging to transform the actual other actor into the character's specific person — simultaneously imaging the chosen person (the choice) while looking at and responding to the real actor, blending the two until they merge into one reality.
endowment_via_imaging.craft_use: Used in 'night, Mother: each actress talks to the other while imaging her own real-life mother/daughter in the other's place. The actress images the choice's expressions, voice, specific mannerisms, and then blends those images with the actual responses received. Technique works to make the hypothetical relationship acceptable as truth.
endowment_via_imaging.when_to_use: When the relationship obligation requires specific personal parallels; when the material calls for specific emotional dynamics (mother-daughter, brothers, romantic partners) that the actor has real-life parallels for.

sources.summary: Night Mother: each actress images her real-life equivalent while talking to the other actress — blend images with real responses.
Technique progressively absorbs hypothetical into real; cumulative unconscious absorption.

time_and_place_imaging.name: Time and Place Imaging
time_and_place_imaging.description: Using imaging — either with Sense Memory or with Imaging Sensations — to create the environment of the material as a genuine sensory reality, so that the actor is truly affected by the place and time as the character would be.
time_and_place_imaging.craft_use: Select a personal place with the same emotional and historical meaning as the play's environment. Create it with all five senses simultaneously; put yourself in it. Choose Imaging with Sense Memory if the place is remote or needs maximum tangibility; choose Imaging Sensations if the place is vivid and the flow should be uninterrupted.
time_and_place_imaging.when_to_use: First in the craft process, or whenever the place is the dominant factor in the material. Re-created at each rehearsal and in each performance.

sources.summary: Select a personal place with the same meaning as the play's; create it with both imaging approaches depending on vividness.
Time and Place stimulates unconscious emotional points of view; actor's relationship to the place affects behavior on multiple levels.

creating_music_as_choice.name: Creating a Piece of Music (Emotional Obligation)
creating_music_as_choice.description: The actor selects a specific piece of music that personally evokes the emotions demanded by the scene, then uses Imaging (typically Imaging Sensations) to hear and feel that music internally while performing.
creating_music_as_choice.instrumental_use: Can be used instrumentally to access and sustain an emotional state for personal healing or emotional liberation exercises.
creating_music_as_choice.craft_use: Used as a choice for the emotional obligation. The actor images the music playing internally throughout the scene, allowing the music's emotional impact to drive organic impulses. Example: actor playing a union organizer images the 'Big Country' theme music to feel heroic grandeur appropriate to the role.
creating_music_as_choice.when_to_use: When the scene demands a sustained emotional quality that the actor has difficulty generating through purely personal memory. Music can be pre-loaded in preparation and then internally maintained during performance.

creating_experience_as_choice.name: Creating an Experience (Emotional Obligation)
creating_experience_as_choice.description: The actor selects a personal past experience that produced the needed emotions and uses Imaging to re-enter that experience sensorially, reliving its emotional impact during the scene.
creating_experience_as_choice.craft_use: Used for the emotional obligation. The actor identifies a past event with the right emotional quality and images it in preparation or during the scene. Example: actress in 'Hearts' who must experience terror of falling uses her real memory of a motorcycle accident, imaging the specific sensory details to re-experience the terror.
creating_experience_as_choice.when_to_use: When the actor has a personal experience with the exact emotional quality needed. Works especially well when the experience is viscerally remembered with strong sensory detail.

creating_person_as_choice.name: Creating a Person (Emotional Obligation)
creating_person_as_choice.description: The actor selects a real or imagined person who evokes the needed emotions and uses Imaging to conjure that person sensorially – typically in two parts: (1) imaging the person to elicit feelings toward them, then (2) Fantasy Story Imaging of the person in a scene-relevant situation.
creating_person_as_choice.craft_use: Two-part technique for the emotional obligation. Part 1: Fragmented Imaging of the person (sensory details of their appearance, sounds, smells, mannerisms) to kindle love, connection, or whatever feeling is needed. Part 2: Fantasy Story Imaging of that person experiencing the scene's parallel situation, e.g., imaging them receiving a letter, to produce the actor's appropriate emotional response. Example: 'Dear Jane' letter scene – actor images Tim (friend) via Fragmented Imaging for love, then Fantasy Story Imaging of Jane reading the letter.
creating_person_as_choice.when_to_use: When the scene involves powerful feelings toward a specific person (love, grief, longing) and the actor can use a real person from their life as a parallel choice.

character_background_story_imaging.name: Character Background Story Imaging
character_background_story_imaging.description: Using Story Imaging (and optionally Fantasy Story Imaging) to construct a series of imagined biographical episodes that establish the character's psychological history prior to the play's action.
character_background_story_imaging.instrumental_use: Can be done as an instrumental exercise when the actor needs to develop empathy for a character whose psychology is foreign to them.
character_background_story_imaging.craft_use: Central technique for the character obligation. The actor creates multiple episodes in chronological order – formative events, relationships, humiliations, joys – that explain why the character is who they are. Each episode is a full Story Imaging experience with all senses engaged. The actor mixes real parallels from their own life with imagined events (Believability), so that the constructed history integrates with unconscious memory.
character_background_story_imaging.when_to_use: During the rehearsal period before staging begins. Especially important for psychologically complex or morally extreme characters (villains, killers, severely traumatized individuals) where the actor's own experience is insufficient.

imaging_craft_technique_endowments.name: Imaging as Craft Technique with Endowments
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.description: Using Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations to sculpt a chosen person's features, voice, smell, texture, and essence onto a scene partner, creating a conglomerate endowment.
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.craft_use: The actor first exhausts Available Stimulus (real similarities between partner and choice), then begins imaging the choice's unique qualities in relation to the partner: hair, skin, eye fleck, nostril shape, breathing sounds, skin texture against cheek, tears in corners of eyes, etc. All five senses work simultaneously. The partner and choice gradually merge into a conglomerate. The actor responds to the partner's real behavior as if it were the choice person's behavior.
imaging_craft_technique_endowments.when_to_use: In rehearsal when the scene requires deep relationship feelings that Available Stimulus alone cannot produce. Most effective when the actor has had an intimate relationship with the chosen person.

imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.name: Imaging as Craft Technique with Affective Memory
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.description: Using Story Imaging + a combination of Sense Memory (sensory questions) and Imaging Sensations to chronologically re-create a traumatic or impactful past event, triggering genuine unconscious reliving of the original emotions.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.instrumental_use: Not primarily instrumental; however, the re-experience can have therapeutic value.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.craft_use: Process: Start with Sense Memory sensory questions in the present tense to build the environment (What do I see? What do I smell? Who else is there?). As specificity deepens and all senses activate simultaneously, transition into Imaging Sensations (the questions cease and images flow automatically). Address all component parts chronologically up to and through the emotional peak. Repeated practice compresses the duration from hours to minutes. The experience then unfolds alongside the written scene in performance.
imaging_craft_technique_affective_memory.when_to_use: When the scene demands an extreme emotional state that the actor has actually experienced in their life. The experience must be specific and well-remembered enough to be reconstructed sensorially.

imaging_subpersonalities_technique.name: Imaging to Access Subpersonalities (Four-Part Process)
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.description: A four-step imaging technique for eliciting specific subpersonalities: (1) image the subpart sensorially in a chosen physical space; (2) step into its energy field and inhabit it; (3) use mirror work to verify and deepen embodiment; (4) image through the subpart's perspective toward character obligations.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.instrumental_use: Used instrumentally to develop an aware ego: the actor learns to recognize, access, and hold the various selves in balance. Helps actors become familiar with primary, opposite, and disowned selves as a prerequisite to using subpersonalities as an acting tool.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.craft_use: Used as the choice approach for accessing the subpersonality that matches the character's dominant energy. The actor images the subpart, inhabits it, and then allows it to carry the performance. Can be done in 8-10 minutes. Any imaging technique (Fragmented, Story, Sensations) can be used within the four-step structure.
imaging_subpersonalities_technique.when_to_use: In preparation before a rehearsal or performance, or during the rehearsal period to establish which subpersonality best serves the material. Prerequisite: familiarity with the subpersonality through prior voice dialogue or other approach techniques.

voice_dialogue_technique.name: Voice Dialogue (Subpersonality Approach Technique)
voice_dialogue_technique.description: A two-person facilitation process in which a facilitator speaks directly with the subject's various subpersonalities, each of which physically moves to a different area of the room and speaks as a distinct entity.
voice_dialogue_technique.instrumental_use: Primary instrumental use: helps the actor identify primary and disowned selves, develop an aware ego, understand how subparts function in their life. Prerequisite for all other subpersonality work. Can visibly transform the subject's physical appearance, posture, voice, and attitude while in a specific self. Facilitator guidelines: maintain benevolent demeanor, never disagree, notice vocal changes and physical cues, start with 'What's going on in your life?' to establish a psychic map of the territory.
voice_dialogue_technique.craft_use: After sufficient experience with instrumental voice dialogue, can be used to elicit the desired subpersonality before or during performance. Requires a skilled facilitator; cannot be done solo.
voice_dialogue_technique.when_to_use: Early in the actor's subpersonality work, before attempting other techniques. Can continue throughout the actor's life as a tool for self-awareness and accessing new or disowned selves.

role_playing_subpersonality.name: Role-Playing (Subpersonality Access Technique)
role_playing_subpersonality.description: Solo technique: the actor achieves an aware-ego state, asks a specific subpart to come forth, moves to a physical space that feels like its location, and encourages it to speak in an uninterrupted monologue.
role_playing_subpersonality.instrumental_use: Allows solo access to subpersonalities without a facilitator. The actor must have prior experience with voice dialogue to know who the selves are. The monologue runs without the actor trying to communicate back to the subpart—conversation would be confusing. If the subpart meanders, the actor returns to an aware-ego state and redirects.
role_playing_subpersonality.craft_use: The actor elicits the subpersonality and then moves directly into the scene or monologue while permeated with that energy. Example: accessing the Inner Critic before performing a monologue adds a judgmental, critical energy to the performance.
role_playing_subpersonality.when_to_use: When a facilitator is not available. Requires considerable prior experience with voice dialogue.

primal_moan_technique.name: Primal Moan (Unconscious Access Technique)
primal_moan_technique.description: Fetal-position primal exercise: deep moaning from the lower abdomen, building in volume and depth, crashing through emotional blocks and soliciting unconscious impulses and images.
primal_moan_technique.instrumental_use: Primary use: break through emotional blocks; access unconscious material; condition the instrument to communicate with the unconscious. Also useful for actors who are cut off or extremely blocked emotionally before rehearsal.
primal_moan_technique.craft_use: When used as preparation for a scene: prime unconscious participation, then transition to working for a choice. The unconscious impulses and images solicited carry over into the scene work.
primal_moan_technique.when_to_use: When the actor is emotionally blocked or disconnected from unconscious impulses. Also as a regular daily practice to build a bridge to the unconscious over time.

primitive_abandonment_technique.name: Primitive Abandonment Exercise
primitive_abandonment_technique.description: Standing large-movement exercise with animal-like sounds, building in rhythmical intensity toward a prehistoric, primal energy that liberates blocked emotions and piques unconscious impulses and disowned subpersonalities.
primitive_abandonment_technique.instrumental_use: Liberates angry, hostile impulses in conflict-phobic actors; frees sexual energy; accesses satanic/demonic subpersonalities; produces primal images including unfamiliar faces (possibly unconscious in origin). Often used as a cluster with Primal Moan and other primitive exercises.
primitive_abandonment_technique.craft_use: When used as preparation: move directly from the exercise into a choice that addresses the material. The unconscious priming carries into the scene. Transition: quiet assimilation/meditative state → choice work.
primitive_abandonment_technique.when_to_use: When the actor needs to access primal, raw, or extremely suppressed emotional energies. Also as a regular practice to condition the unconscious bridge.

disconnected_impulsivity_technique.name: Disconnected Impulsivity
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.description: High-speed, non-logically-connected expression of impulses, images, and associations with no premeditation and no commentary between responses, used to antidote concept/leadership and to access unconscious material.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.instrumental_use: Primary: antidote to conceptual acting and preplanning. Secondary: piquing unconscious responses. After completing the exercise, do Personal Inventory to assess the emotional state. Meaningful items that arose can be used as starting points for a second, directed run (Adding Meaningful Suggestions variant).
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.craft_use: Used as a preparation before a scene: having identified the desired emotional state, the actor suggests a flow of images related to a particular time or issue, expecting them to hook into the unconscious and support the scene's emotional obligations.
disconnected_impulsivity_technique.when_to_use: When an actor is intellectually controlling their work, or when they need to prime the unconscious before rehearsal or performance. Daily or weekly practice recommended.

logy_exercise.name: Logy Exercise (Hypnagogic State Induction)
logy_exercise.description: The actor lies prone, exaggerates body weight to slothlike heaviness while making the sound 'logy,' deliberately approaching the sleep threshold to induce the hypnagogic state and access unconscious images.
logy_exercise.instrumental_use: Induces the hypnagogic state—the threshold between waking and sleep where the unconscious liberates images. The actor makes a sustained sound using 'logy' through most of the exercise and stops when entering the hypnagogic state. Duration: up to 30 minutes. Actors return looking as if they had awakened from impacting dreams—permissive, benevolent, ready to work. Risk: falling fully asleep. Trick: keep coming out of the hypnagogic state and re-entering it. With repetition, the actor becomes facile at 'working' the unconscious from this state.
logy_exercise.craft_use: Used as a preparation just before rehearsal: enlists unconscious cooperation, then transition directly into choice work for the material.
logy_exercise.when_to_use: As a regular preparation before rehearsal or as a dedicated daily practice to build a bridge to the unconscious. Also: every night as you go to sleep, to build facility with the hypnagogic state over time.

unconscious_suggestions_technique.name: Unconscious Suggestions (Systematic Place Imaging)
unconscious_suggestions_technique.description: Systematic imaging of specific categories of life environments (bedrooms, schoolrooms, religious spaces, playgrounds) to liberate unconscious impulses associated with formative memories.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.instrumental_use: Used as a regular exercise to deepen unconscious access. In workshops: one hour of Unconscious Suggestions immediately before a monologue produces remarkable depth and inspiration. The actor must not comment, acknowledge, or evaluate impulses—just express them. Key rule: When images feel disconnected from the environment—that's a sign the unconscious is responding. DO NOT COMMENT. Just express.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.craft_use: Can be used as a preparation to prime specific emotional states if the chosen environment is known to contain impetus relevant to the material's obligations.
unconscious_suggestions_technique.when_to_use: As part of a regular daily or weekly unconscious-access practice. Also immediately before a rehearsal or performance when the actor needs deep emotional connection.

active_imagination_technique.name: Active Imagination (Dream Work Technique)
active_imagination_technique.description: Consciously dialoguing with dream figures by placing them in a chair, writing in stream-of-consciousness, or moving expressively—as a way of receiving unconscious messages from dream content.
active_imagination_technique.instrumental_use: Primary: establish communication with unconscious content from dreams. Ask the dream figure questions; receive surprising answers from the unconscious. Determine whether the figure is subjective (represents a part of self) or objective (represents another person in life). Use in combination with Imaging: create the figure through Imaging Sensations while dialoguing, making them more tangible and increasing unconscious access.
active_imagination_technique.craft_use: Can be used to deepen a dream-based choice: active imagination with the key symbols of the dream builds a richer, more tangible inner reality for the character. Particularly effective when combined with Symbolic Imaging.
active_imagination_technique.when_to_use: After recording and re-reading a dream; when the dream contains significant but unclear symbols or figures. Also: when the actor wants to extend the working life of a dream beyond its original imaging.

dream_re_creation_imaging.name: Re-Creating the Dream Through Imaging
dream_re_creation_imaging.description: Using Imaging techniques (Symbolic, Fragmented, Story, Verbalized, or Imaging Sensations) to re-create a recorded dream in full sensory detail, establishing a direct conscious-unconscious connection.
dream_re_creation_imaging.instrumental_use: Begin with the dream's symbols (identify by re-reading journal, noting words that feel symbolic). Start with Symbolic Imaging, jumping from object to object randomly. Then use Imaging Sensations to fill in surrounding elements and build a frame around the key symbols. Use active imagination to dialogue with dream figures. Draw or sculpt dream figures as an additional catapult. Repeat several times; each repetition deepens the connection.
dream_re_creation_imaging.craft_use: Use the re-created dream as a preparation for a scene whose emotional obligations match the dream's emotional content. After dream re-creation, move directly into working for the choice.
dream_re_creation_imaging.when_to_use: When a dream seems impacting and emotionally significant. Do not need to re-create every dream—only the most vivid and memorable. The dream selected for re-creation should contain enough sensory and emotional detail to be workable.

perspective_inventory_technique.name: Perspective Inventory (Technique)
perspective_inventory_technique.description: A progressive multi-week exercise that systematically walks backward through the actor's life, week by week, month by month, year by year, to recover early childhood memories and unlock unconscious material.
perspective_inventory_technique.instrumental_use: Works as a primer/lever to pry open sealed memory boxes. Each meaningful remembered event brings surrounding memories with it. Progressively opens access to experiences from age 3 and younger—material that may include prenatal responses. Used as a foundation for 'I'm five years old and I…' work. Actors have reported remembering their birth through this process.
perspective_inventory_technique.craft_use: Not primarily a craft technique; primarily instrumental/developmental.
perspective_inventory_technique.when_to_use: As a regular weekly practice over months. Also: when doing the Abstract Places from Childhood area of Unconscious Suggestions.

programming_the_unconscious_technique.name: Programming the Unconscious (Six-Step Process)
programming_the_unconscious_technique.description: A comprehensive pre-performance preparation process in which the actor systematically images character-parallel experiences from their own life, using repetition, dreams, hypnagogic states, and journals to imprint those experiences in the unconscious—so the unconscious will support the character organically in performance.
programming_the_unconscious_technique.instrumental_use: Also instrumentally valuable for deepening the actor's connection to the unconscious and developing a richer life of choices over time.
programming_the_unconscious_technique.craft_use: Step 1: Read the play (3+ times: objective → dynamics → character). Start mini-journal. Step 2: Create pre-play experiences: image what happened before the play begins    and between scenes, using personal parallels (selectively emphasized). Step 3: Image all character relationships: find personal parallels for each character,    build Fragmented Imaging montages of parallel relationship histories. Step 4: Address written scenes: chart character's evolution scene by scene;    create imaging parallel for each scene using personal experiences. Step 5: Image yourself successfully moving through all character experiences with    great involvement; repeat many times; encourage into hypnagogic state and dreams. Step 6: Keep programming journal throughout. Also use: Walter Mitty Option (fantasy parallels), Symbolic Imaging of dream symbols as performance triggers, Endowments to support programming when working with other actors.
programming_the_unconscious_technique.when_to_use: Only for major, multifaceted roles. Begin several weeks before rehearsal if possible. Requires psychological stability and well-developed eleventh level of consciousness.

deprogramming_technique.name: Deprogramming the Unconscious Technique
