By re-creating your own parallel experiences of those forces, you build an organic unconscious foundation for the character's distorted perception of himself and the world.
willy_loman_unconscious_preparation.additional_info: Morris notes: it is not that the actor IS like the character—it is that by selectively isolating and emphasizing the parallel experiences, they create an emotional DNA that mirrors the character's. Quote: 'I'm not John Jones, but I am! Everybody is!'

dream_journal_four_section_entry.name: Four-Section Dream Journal Entry
dream_journal_four_section_entry.goal: Create a complete, structured dream journal entry that transforms a raw dream into an analyzed, translated, and re-created acting resource.
dream_journal_four_section_entry.how_to: SECTION 1 – DESCRIBE: Write the entire dream as soon as you awaken. Record exactly: what happened, who was there, what they did, what you felt, in what order. Leave nothing out, even if it seems trivial. SECTION 2 – INTERPRET: Identify all symbols and symbolic behaviors. List all feelings and reactions—yours and others'. Determine: are the people subjective (parts of you) or objective (others in your life)? Use Active Imagination: put each key figure in a chair, ask them questions. Check for connections to your waking life. Check for disowned subpersonalities seeking recognition. SECTION 3 – TRANSLATE: List the emotional components and the parts of yourself the dream reveals. Identify acting choices: what material obligations could this dream address? Note which of its energy states could serve as subpersonality choices. SECTION 4 – RE-CREATE (choose one or more): A. Original perspective: re-create the dream as you dreamt it (Story/Fragmented Imaging). B. Alternative perspective: inhabit a different dream figure. C. Objective perspective: observe the dream as a detached witness. Write the results of each re-creation in the journal.
dream_journal_four_section_entry.who_should_do_it: All actors doing dream work and Programming the Unconscious.
dream_journal_four_section_entry.why_it_works: The structured approach prevents the actor from treating dreams as mere curiosities. Each section extracts a different type of value: description preserves the raw material; interpretation reveals the unconscious message; translation creates actionable acting tools; re-creation builds the conscious-unconscious bridge.
dream_journal_four_section_entry.additional_info: Marty's dream journal example: Description: fight behind barn, actor in hayloft watching. Interpretation: Witness State, killer/warrior energies in combat. Translation: access warrior energy for violent characters. Re-creation A: Fragmented Imaging from hayloft perspective—actor felt excited but uninvolved. Re-creation B: Assumed identity of one fighter—felt like killer AND victim; yelled and screamed.

glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.name: Glass Menagerie Dream Journal Entries Exercise
glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.goal: Demonstrate how to use actual dreams generated by programming workouts as additional programming material.
glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.how_to: 1. After each imaging parallel workout, record any dreams the following morning. 2. First entry example: Actor dreams he has the body of a little boy yet is an adult—cannot reach the tabletop; mother ignores his plea for help. 3. Interpret: being too small = helplessness (Tom's core state). 4. Second entry: image the dream—create a small body, walk around in one's real home enlarging objects. (Result: found it amusing rather than affecting.) 5. Third entry: image the dream again but set it in the play's environment. (Result: felt abandoned and unheard—successful.) 6. Record each result and iterate.
glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.who_should_do_it: Actors actively programming the unconscious for a specific role.
glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.why_it_works: Dreams generated by programming workouts contain the unconscious's own interpretation and amplification of the parallel material, providing new imagery and emotional depth.
glass_menagerie_dream_journal_entries.additional_info: From The Glass Menagerie programming example (Tom character). Dreams with disowned subpersonalities are especially valuable—the threatening figure may be the character's disowned self.

glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.name: Glass Menagerie Walter Mitty Option — Scene 1 Fantasy Imaging Exercise
glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.goal: Create total character reality for Tom (Glass Menagerie, Scene 1) entirely through Fantasy Story Imaging when no exact personal parallel is available.
glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.how_to: 1. Sit in the rehearsal space; use Story Imaging to create the scene's physical environment: the cardboard lampshade, discolored walls, old wallpaper. 2. Image Amanda (using the actress or a substitute) as your mother; image Laura as your sister. 3. Use all five senses: smell the spaghetti, hear Amanda's Southern drawl, feel the claustrophobia. 4. Follow the written scene verbatim in fantasy (replicate the dialogue in imagination). 5. Create your emotional responses organically from the images—your love for Laura, your irritation at Amanda. 6. Repeat until the fantasy becomes an indistinguishable reality.
glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.who_should_do_it: Any actor who cannot find a sufficient real-life parallel for a character's situation.
glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.why_it_works: With repetition, fantasy and truth become intermingled; the unconscious accepts repeated imagery as real, creating genuine belief in the circumstances.
glass_menagerie_walter_mitty_scene_one.additional_info: Three substitution options for the other characters: (a) use the actual co-actors, (b) substitute real people from your life, (c) use entirely imagined/fictitious people.

deprogramming_exercise.name: Deprogramming the Unconscious Exercise
deprogramming_exercise.goal: Systematically clear all programming imagery from the unconscious after completing a role, restoring the actor's authentic reality.
deprogramming_exercise.how_to: 1. Before opening the journal: recite the mantra aloud many times: 'I worked on [character] for artistic reasons. I am not like [character]. My life is different and healthier.' 2. Open the programming journal to the first entry. Read it aloud, remembering the images created. 3. Objectify each parallel against reality: contrast every invented or exaggerated element with the actual truth of your life. Say these contrasts aloud. 4. If needed, use imaging to re-create your true reality—your real home, real mother, real relationships as they actually are—to implant accurate images. 5. Work through every entry in order. Repeat over an entire week if necessary. 6. Use the hypnagogic state to plant antidotal images; order counter-dreams. 7. Know you are done when the programming no longer has emotional pull.
deprogramming_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors who used unconscious programming for a role, once the production closes.
deprogramming_exercise.why_it_works: The unconscious accepts repeated, sincere imagery as reality. By implanting the truth with the same deliberateness used to create parallels, the actor resets the unconscious. The body's homeostatic function supports this reset naturally.
deprogramming_exercise.additional_info: The mantra functions like reprogramming a computer—downloading new information to replace old conditions. The unconscious is described as brilliant and naturally truth-seeking.

long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.name: Long Day's Journey Jamie — Unconscious Suggestions for Dark Character Exercise
long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.goal: Stimulate relevant dark dreams and unconscious connections for a character with heavy illness, mortality, and family trauma themes.
long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.how_to: 1. Study the character (Jamie, Long Day's Journey into Night): tuberculosis, miserly penny-pinching father, drug-addicted mother, financial desperation. 2. Identify the darkest areas of unconscious suggestions from your own life: people who have died, funerals, shadow figures, experiences with sick people and hospitals. 3. Image those specific suggestions repeatedly at the right times (before sleep). 4. Record all resulting dreams and study them for connections to the character's reality. 5. Use the dream imagery as parallel material in programming workouts.
long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.who_should_do_it: Actors approaching characters whose core experiences are dark, traumatic, or illness-related.
long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.why_it_works: Specific unconscious suggestions activate relevant memory clusters and stimulate dreams that the unconscious generates in its own interpretive language.
long_nights_journey_unconscious_suggesti.additional_info: Reference play: Eugene O'Neill, Long Day's Journey into Night.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to begin learning imaging
recommendations.recommendation: Start by becoming conscious of what imaging you already do naturally: what kinds of images, when they occur, what patterns emerge. Then practice Imaging with Sense Memory using real places or events from your life, always opening the eyes once the inner image is established.

sources.summary: Begin by becoming aware of existing imaging patterns; image with eyes open after starting with eyes closed.
Start with an environment; open eyes shortly after beginning; involve all senses.

recommendations.condition: Actor is visually dominant and neglects other senses in imaging
recommendations.recommendation: Deliberately begin Imaging Sensations exercises with a non-visual sense (smell, sound, or touch). Practice giving the non-visual senses the same total emphasis you'd give the visual. Over-correct until all senses become habitual.

sources.summary: To overcome visual dominance, initially use non-visual senses with greater emphasis than visual.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use imaging as a choice approach in a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the scene obligation, select a choice from your own life that would stimulate the appropriate emotional response, then create that choice through Imaging with Sense Memory. Work with all five senses simultaneously, become part of the image (do not watch it on a screen), and let the images run free between sensorial explorations.

sources.summary: Become part of the image — do not watch it on a screen.
Image with outer eyes open; involve all five senses; let the image flow.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access deeper, unconsciously rooted emotional life for a scene
recommendations.recommendation: During rehearsal, work for your conscious choice while carefully noting all involuntary images that arise. After the session, re-create those involuntary images using the Imaging technique — they likely represent the unconscious's own translation of your obligation and will produce richer emotional life than your original choice.

sources.summary: Re-creating involuntary images produces more dimensional emotional life than original choices.
Involuntary images are the unconscious's translation of the conscious choice.

recommendations.condition: Actor uses Sense Memory alone and finds it overly analytical
recommendations.recommendation: Combine Sense Memory with Imaging: establish the overall environment through imaging first, then use sensorial questions to deepen specific elements. Allow the image to run freely between questions rather than remaining in pure question-response mode.

sources.summary: Imaging+Sense Memory combination is richer than either alone; image provides flow while SM provides specificity.

recommendations.condition: Actor is doing sleep-wake state work, free association, or unconscious programming and finds sensorial questioning intrusive
recommendations.recommendation: Switch from Imaging with Sense Memory to Imaging Sensations — allow all senses to respond simultaneously without the question-response structure, preserving the uninterrupted flow needed for these altered-state techniques.

sources.summary: Imaging Sensations is appropriate when sensorial questioning would interrupt the altered state needed for unconscious programming or free association.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants a reliable shortcut to specific emotional states in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Build a personal library of symbolic images through journal practice. Identify symbols that consistently produce specific feeling states. Use these as shortcut triggers — re-creating the symbol in the moment of need rather than working through a full choice approach.

sources.summary: Symbolic images can serve as reliable emotional triggers once catalogued and tested.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to quickly elevate vulnerability or emotional availability before a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging with a personal theme (loss, love, childhood) that you know affects you. Let images tumble without needing them to connect. Steer toward the impacting moments using Selective Emphasis. Works much faster than Story Imaging.

sources.summary: Fragmented Imaging: fast, can address all obligations, uses Selective Emphasis; vulnerability example given.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps anticipating and guiding his own choice exploration (self-facilitation bias)
recommendations.recommendation: Switch to Guided Imaging with a scene partner. Because you cannot predict the facilitator's questions, you explore the choice without premeditation — discovering what the choice actually does to you rather than what you expect it to do.

sources.summary: Guided Imaging frees the actor from anticipatory bias — facilitatee cannot predict questions.

recommendations.condition: Scene partners have a long shared history that does not appear in the script
recommendations.recommendation: Use Shared Imaging to build that history. One actor shares images of relevant personal experiences; the other images exactly what is described (not parallels). Do this scene-by-scene, also covering extra-textual life between scenes. Consider doing sessions in a hypnagogic state to encourage the shared material to enter dreams.

sources.summary: Shared Imaging builds shared history including extra-textual life; hypnagogic state deepens the bond.

recommendations.condition: Actor arrives at performance depressed, stressed, or emotionally shut down
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fantasy Story Imaging — find a topic that has always been pleasurably memorable (something that speeds up your heartbeat), then create a fully multi-sensory fantasy story based on it. Allow the emotional response to build and amplify. The clown/circus example demonstrates the transformation from depression to joy and love.

sources.summary: Fantasy Imaging transforms depression through pleasurably memorable topic and full sensory Story Image.

recommendations.condition: Actor has a persistent fear (auditions, exposure, ridicule, conflict) that keeps returning
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a daily imaging practice for fear elimination: (1) re-experience the fear's origins fully through sensory imaging until the source is clear; (2) change the image — rewrite the outcome to one of power, success, and confidence. Do this repeatedly over weeks or months. This reaches the unconscious where the fear was originally encoded and reprograms it.

sources.summary: Two-step fear elimination process: re-experience then change the image; works on the unconscious encoding of the fear.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use Verbalized Imaging but keeps describing images in words rather than experiencing them
recommendations.recommendation: Watch for the distinction: verbal description short-circuits the sensory process. When you verbalize, use minimal language as a trigger ('I see...', 'I smell...') and then let the sense respond sensorially. After verbalizing, reduce and eventually eliminate the words as the imaging becomes self-sustaining. Verbalize only to START the process and to stay on track.

sources.summary: When verbalizing images, do NOT respond in words — keep sensory responses non-verbal or the process short-circuits.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty starting the imaging process
recommendations.recommendation: Use Verbalized Imaging as a starting mechanism: begin by acknowledging the difficulty aloud ('I feel reluctant to start...I'm not sure where to begin...I'm afraid it won't work...'), then sneak into imaging by simply starting 'I see...' and filling in the first thing that comes. The acknowledgment of obstacles clears them so the imaging can begin.

sources.summary: Start Verbalized Imaging by acknowledging the obstacles aloud, then begin 'I see...' — the acknowledgment clears the block.

recommendations.condition: Actor is blocked on the spot before a performance or class and cannot reach a BEING state
recommendations.recommendation: Use I'm Five Years Old imaging: start the prompt 'I'm five years old and I...' and respond with images, not words. Progress through ages. This simultaneously creates selfless involvement, elevates affectability, and piques unconscious memories — tripling the instrumental benefit in a single exercise.

sources.summary: I'm Five Years Old imaging variant: triple benefit for on-the-spot instrumental work.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to build an authentic relationship with a scene partner quickly
recommendations.recommendation: Layer three approaches: (1) Shared Imaging — each shares personal experiences that parallel the play's circumstances; (2) Guided Imaging — facilitate each other through relevant personal experiences without anticipatory bias; (3) Relationship Imaging — while relating to the real partner, silently image modifications in their behavior. Progress to Endowment via Imaging once a foundation is established.

sources.summary: Multiple relationship imaging techniques layered to build authentic relationship.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build the unconscious background of a character between rehearsals
recommendations.recommendation: Practice Parallel Imaging daily: image yourself living the character's life using the play's actual circumstances. Weave in personal parallels where they resemble the character's experiences. Do this before sleep or in the sleep-wake state for faster unconscious absorption. Keep a journal of which images produce the most impact.

sources.summary: Parallel Imaging for unconscious character background: image oneself in the character's life; personal parallels; absorbs into unconscious.

recommendations.condition: Actor's imagination feels weak, overly literal, or unable to generate creative choices
recommendations.recommendation: Do daily imagination-elevating exercises: Object Imaging (give an ordinary object a complete life and story), Impulsive Object Imaging (allow bizarre, illogical images from ordinary objects), Free-Association Imaging, and Fantasy Imaging. When images become outrageous or nonsensical, celebrate — it means the imagination is hooking into the unconscious.

sources.summary: Bizarre/illogical images in Object Imaging indicate the imagination is beginning to hook into the unconscious.

recommendations.condition: Actor is addressing the time and place obligation and the environment is unfamiliar
recommendations.recommendation: Select a personal place that carries the same emotional and historical meaning as the play's environment — not just visually similar. Consider the character's history in that place. Create it through Imaging with Sense Memory if it is remote; use Imaging Sensations if it is vivid. Put yourself in it; surround yourself with the sensory elements; let the emotional response come from the place itself.

sources.summary: Time and place selection must carry the same meaning to the actor as the play's environment carries to the character; create with full sensory immersion.

recommendations.condition: A scene requires an emotional transition between two distinct states at a specific moment
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging transitionally: establish the first emotional state with positive sensory fragments in preparation, then at the moment of shift, rapidly generate contrasting fragments (e.g., from bright eyes/laughter → sad eyes/stooped shoulders/gray skin). No words—only sensory flashes. The contrast drives the organic shift.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to fulfill the emotional obligation for a scene requiring heroic grandeur or a specific emotional quality they cannot generate from memory alone
recommendations.recommendation: Select a piece of music that personally evokes the required emotion and use Imaging Sensations to hear it internally during the scene. Pre-load the music in preparation so it can be maintained internally through performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor must play a character with a psychologically extreme or morally alien background (villain, severe trauma, etc.)
recommendations.recommendation: Build a character background through a series of 5-10 Story Imaging episodes, starting at age five and moving chronologically through formative events. Mix real parallels from your life with imagined events (Believability) so the history integrates with your unconscious. Focus each episode on experiences that create the specific emotional underpinnings (rejection, humiliation, rage) that explain the character's behavior.

recommendations.condition: Actor is building a complex, long-standing character relationship (e.g., father-son over decades)
recommendations.recommendation: Use Fragmented Imaging + Imaging Sensations across multiple selectively emphasized episodes from your relationship with a parallel person in your life. Cover at least 10-15 episodes across different ages and contexts. Begin with Available Stimulus exploration of your scene partner to establish the Endowment foundation simultaneously.

recommendations.condition: Actor is using Affective Memory and the conventional Sense Memory approach feels insufficient to trigger the full emotional response
recommendations.recommendation: Transition from Sense Memory sensory questions to Imaging Sensations mid-process: start with chronological sensory questions (Sense Memory approach) to build the environment, then as all senses begin working simultaneously, allow the questions to cease and let Story Imaging Sensations take over. Repeat the entire Affective Memory process multiple times to compress it and deepen reliability.

recommendations.condition: Scene partner is not stimulating the relationship feelings demanded by the material
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaging as craft technique with Endowments: after exhausting Available Stimulus (real similarities between partner and your choice), use Fragmented Imaging to sculpt your choice's unique sensory qualities onto the partner. Create a conglomerate endowment so that the partner's actual behavior registers as coming from your choice person.

recommendations.condition: A choice approach (e.g., Endowments, Affective Memory, Objects That Come into Contact with the Body) lacks its own built-in technique
recommendations.recommendation: Use Imaging as the craft technique. Along with Sense Memory and Sensory Suggestion, Imaging can serve as the approach technique for any choice approach that does not have a built-in process. Imaging Sensations is the most versatile form for this purpose.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use subpersonalities as an acting tool but does not yet know their own selves
recommendations.recommendation: Begin with instrumental voice dialogue with a qualified facilitator. Identify primary selves and disowned selves, develop an aware ego. Only after developing familiarity with the selves should you attempt to use subpersonalities as a craft choice approach. Supplement with journal writing, dreams, and role-playing solo.

recommendations.condition: Actor must play a character who is clearly dominated by a specific energy (obsessive achiever, dictator, victim, warrior, etc.)
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the character's dominant subpersonality, access that self using the most appropriate technique (voice dialogue, imaging, role-playing), and allow it to carry the character's entire sense of life. The subpersonality will know how to respond to other characters without intellectual direction. For secondary relationships, identify which self the character inhabits when relating to each other person and access that self accordingly.

recommendations.condition: Actor is emotionally blocked before rehearsal or performance and cannot access deep feeling
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Primal Moan or Primitive Abandonment as preparation. Assume the fetal position and moan from the lower abdomen (Primal Moan), building progressively louder and deeper. Or use Primitive Abandonment standing, with large animal sounds and total physical commitment. After either exercise, allow quiet assimilation, then move directly into choice work for the material.

recommendations.condition: Actor's work consistently feels shallow, indicates disconnection from unconscious resources
recommendations.recommendation: Establish a daily practice of Personal Inventory, Nonverbal Personal Inventory, and Personal Point of View to elevate consciousness and invite unconscious impulses. Note any abstract or mysterious responses that surface and explore them via Imaging. Add Primal Moan and Primitive Abandonment several times a week. Begin dream journaling and studying the messages of disowned selves.

recommendations.condition: An unconscious impulse surfaces spontaneously during Personal Inventory and seems significant
recommendations.recommendation: Deliberately re-create that impulse through Imaging (Fragmented, Story, or Verbalized). Start anywhere in the experience, allow images to run out, do not edit impulses. Repeat several times. Each re-creation brings the unconscious material closer to conscious awareness and widens the channel.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to access a nonverbal or primal subpersonality that does not respond to verbal techniques
recommendations.recommendation: Use Movement/Dance, Externals (Animals), Drawing/Sculpture, or Primitive Abandonment to access these selves. Experiment with animals that match the needed energy: apes/gorillas for primitive/aggressive selves; big cats for predator energy; reptiles for satanic/instinctual energy; birds vary widely. If solo, use stream-of-consciousness drawing or clay sculpting while in that energy.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use a vivid or recurring dream as unconscious material for their work
recommendations.recommendation: Record the dream immediately upon awakening. Do not analyze it—re-create it through Imaging starting from the beginning, using all five senses in a relaxed state. At moments of high emotional intensity, stay with and encourage the feelings. Repeat several times. If using as an acting preparation, move directly from the dream re-creation into choice work for the material.

recommendations.condition: Actor is stuck in conceptual or preplanned responses and cannot access organic moment-to-moment impulses
recommendations.recommendation: Use Disconnected Impulsivity (one-person or two-person Switch-Trick). Express impulses at high speed with no logical connections and no premeditation. After the exercise, do Personal Inventory. Note any meaningful items that arose and use them as starting suggestions for a second run (Adding Meaningful Suggestions variant). Practice daily or weekly.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to prime the unconscious as preparation for a rehearsal or performance
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Logy exercise to deliberately induce the hypnagogic state. Lie prone, exaggerate body weight, make 'logy' sounds, approach the sleep threshold. When images arise, be aware of them without directing them. After the exercise, move directly into working for the choice that addresses the material. Alternatively: use one hour of Unconscious Suggestions (Bedrooms, Schoolrooms, etc.) before a monologue or scene.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a regular daily practice for deepening unconscious connection
recommendations.recommendation: Establish a rotation of: Personal Inventory + Nonverbal Personal Inventory (daily), Disconnected Impulsivity (daily or several times a week), Logy/hypnagogic practice (nightly as you fall asleep), Dream journal (every morning), Unconscious Suggestions cluster (weekly – bedrooms, schoolrooms, playgrounds, religious experiences), Fears Imaging (periodically), Night Games (bedtime fantasy journeys). Treat this as a permanent life practice, not a temporary exercise.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps dismissing spontaneous inner voices and images during rehearsal or performance
recommendations.recommendation: Train as a Professional Experiencer: practice acknowledging every inner voice and image during Personal Inventory work. Express them without editing. In scene work: allow any inner voice or image to influence your behavior through the lines. The audience will discover the next moment at the same time you do—which is when unconscious inspiration makes acting transcendent.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a psychologically complex character whose inner life seems 'beyond their emotional range'
recommendations.recommendation: Identify the character's dominant psychological theme (suppression, abandonment, rage, etc.). Look in your dream journal for a dream that shares that theme. Re-create the dream through Imaging as preparation. If no such dream exists, order one up: think about the theme throughout the day and give yourself a pre-sleep suggestion before bed. Supplement the dream choice with Active Imagination to dialogue with its key figures.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot remember early childhood memories that might be relevant to their work
recommendations.recommendation: Begin Perspective Inventory: review yesterday, then expand one week at a time, progressing backward through your life over multiple weeks. Supplement with Abstract Places from Childhood imaging using the Logy exercise as preparation. Use 'I'm five years old and I…' to anchor specific ages. Allow abstract, discontinuous fragments to arise without seeking logical continuity.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to build a repertoire of choices for future use
recommendations.recommendation: Keep a dream journal—record every morning without fail. Work through the full Unconscious Suggestions cluster (bedrooms, schoolrooms, toys, joys, romances, religious experiences, playgrounds, fragments, abstract places). After each imaging session, note which experiences produced the richest emotional responses—these are your most powerful unconscious-based choices.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps having recurring dreams that feel significant but cannot identify their meaning
recommendations.recommendation: Re-create the dream through Imaging using Symbolic Imaging for the key objects. Apply Active Imagination: place each major figure in the dream in a chair and dialogue with them. Note whether the figure is subjective (part of yourself) or objective (represents another person). Cross-reference the dream's theme with what is happening in your life at present. Share the dream in a group/workshop context for additional insight.

recommendations.condition: Actor has been cast in a major role and has weeks before rehearsal begins
recommendations.recommendation: Begin Programming the Unconscious immediately: 1. Read the play three times (objective → dynamics → character). Start mini-journal. 2. Identify parallel people for each character relationship. 3. Use Fragmented and Story Imaging to create pre-play character history. 4. Begin dream journal; encourage dreams related to the character. 5. Image before sleep using hypnagogic state. 6. Keep programming journal. Use Walter Mitty Option when no real parallel is available.

recommendations.condition: Actor cannot find a real parallel experience for a specific scene's emotional demands
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Walter Mitty Option: create a totally imaginary experience using Imaging (Story or Fragmented), centering yourself in the action repeatedly until the unconscious accepts the imagined event as real. Supplement with Believability (real elements mixed with fantasy). Repeat the imaging many times over multiple sessions.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to use a dream as a performance trigger during a scene
recommendations.recommendation: Identify a strong symbol from the dream (an object, image, or figure). Use Symbolic Imaging to create that symbol as a flash image during the scene. The symbol, having been thoroughly worked in the dream journal and re-creation process, will act as a trigger that liberates the associated unconscious emotion and dimension into the performance.

recommendations.condition: Actor is playing a character whose psychology does not seem to match their own personality at all
recommendations.recommendation: Use Selective Emphasis (John Jones principle): identify the parallel experiences in your own life that DID produce similar emotional underpinnings to the character's, then isolate and enhance ONLY those—deliberately excluding all experiences that produced different or contradictory traits. Remember: we all possess all universal subparts—every character's psychology has a parallel thread somewhere in any human life.

recommendations.condition: Actor has completed Programming the Unconscious and is now in performance
recommendations.recommendation: Trust the foundation that has been built. The unconscious has been imprinted with the parallel experiences. In performance: allow unconscious impulses and inner voices to surface and include them in your behavior through the lines. Use symbolic images from your dream work as flash triggers. The programming will continue to deepen as the run continues.

recommendations.condition: When using the programming process to prepare a role
recommendations.recommendation: Use Step 5 (Imaging Yourself Playing the Part) at any point during the programming process: stand outside yourself and see yourself as the character moving through every environment of the play with total organic reality. Story Imaging works best. This builds confidence and clarifies the character's through-line.

recommendations.condition: When using the hypnagogic state for character programming
recommendations.recommendation: Prepare a specific, pre-planned image before lying down. When the hypnagogic moment arrives, deploy it immediately—there are only a few seconds. Images can be literal, abstract, or symbolic (e.g., for feeling trapped: being bound in a small room, or inside a glass cylinder filling with water). Practice elongating the window. Use the same approach with antidotal images during deprogramming.

recommendations.condition: When integrating dreams into the programming process
recommendations.recommendation: Follow the seven-step blueprint: record all dreams, study them for content and symbols, use dream imagery as character choices, repeatedly re-create significant dreams, plant seeds in the hypnagogic state, watch for disowned subpersonalities appearing in dreams, and steer any lucid dreams into the character's world.

recommendations.condition: When encountering a lucid dream during a programming period
recommendations.recommendation: Gently and lightly steer the dream toward the play's environment and the character's emotional content. Do not be heavy-handed or the dream will break. If this occurs repeatedly, it indicates a strong unconscious-conscious connection is forming.

recommendations.condition: When beginning any unconscious programming journey
recommendations.recommendation: Cultivate and verify a strong eleventh level of consciousness before starting. Know from the outset what you are doing and why. A well-developed eleventh level of consciousness is the safety mechanism that keeps the process creative and sane. If at any time the process does not feel right, stop and adjust.

recommendations.condition: When finishing a production where unconscious programming was used
recommendations.recommendation: Begin the deprogramming process immediately. Use the programming journal to work through every entry in order, contrasting each imaging parallel with your actual reality. Use the mantra repeatedly ('I am not this character; I worked on him for artistic reasons'). Implant antidotal images in the hypnagogic state and order counter-dreams. Trust the unconscious's homeostatic function to support the reset.

recommendations.condition: When no real-life parallel exists for character circumstances
recommendations.recommendation: Apply the Walter Mitty option with Fantasy Story Imaging as a complete alternative. Follow the written scene verbatim in imagination. Use all five senses. Substitute the co-actors, real people, or entirely fictitious people for the other characters. Mix fantasy and reality elements freely. With repetition, the fantasy reality becomes indistinguishable from real experience and programs the unconscious equally effectively.

recommendations.condition: When the actor works with dark or traumatic characters
recommendations.recommendation: Use targeted unconscious suggestions that align with the character's darkest experiences (illness, death, impoverishment, abandonment) to stimulate relevant dreams. Image these suggestions repeatedly before sleep. Record all resulting dreams and use their imagery as character parallels or direct programming material.
